1.title: Tens and Ones
1.tip: Every two-digit number is made of TENS and ONES. In the number 14, there is 1 ten and 4 ones. Think of it like bundling ten sticks together — that bundle is one ten! Understanding place value is the key to working with bigger numbers.

question.text: How many tens are in the number 37?
question.explanation: The digit 3 is in the tens place, so there are 3 tens (which equals 30).

1.title: Subtracting Within 20
1.tip: Subtraction means finding the difference. One handy trick is to count back on a number line. For a number like 12 − 5, start at 12 and jump back 2 to reach 10, then jump back 3 more. Use the same "jump to 10 first" trick to tackle the question below!

question.text: What is 15 − 7?
question.explanation: 15 − 7 = 8! Jump back from 15: subtract 5 to get 10, then subtract 2 more to get 8.

1.title: Describing 2D Shapes
1.tip: We can describe 2D (flat) shapes by their features! Count the SIDES (straight edges) and CORNERS (where sides meet). A rectangle has 4 sides and 4 corners. A pentagon has 5 sides. A hexagon has 6 sides. Circles have NO sides or corners — they are curved!

question.text: How many corners does a rectangle have?
question.explanation: 4 corners! A rectangle has 4 straight sides that meet at 4 corners (right angles).

1.title: Measuring with Informal Units
1.tip: We can measure how long things are using everyday objects! You might measure a table in hand spans, a room in footsteps, or a book in paper clips. Always line up your units carefully from one end to the other without gaps or overlaps!

question.text: A desk is 12 hand spans long. What does this tell us?
question.explanation: The length of the desk is 12 hand spans! We used hand spans as an informal unit of measurement.

1.title: Number and Shape Patterns
1.tip: Patterns follow a rule that repeats or grows! Shape pattern: triangle, circle, triangle, circle — the rule is "alternate." Number pattern: 2, 4, 6, 8 — the rule is "add 2 each time." Finding the rule lets you predict what comes next!

question.text: What comes next in the pattern: 3, 6, 9, 12, …?
question.explanation: 15! The rule is "add 3 each time." 12 + 3 = 15.

1.title: Halves and Quarters
1.tip: A HALF means 2 equal parts — each part is ½. A QUARTER means 4 equal parts — each part is ¼. The parts MUST be equal! If you cut a sandwich unevenly, those are NOT halves. Two quarters (¼ + ¼) make one half (½). Four quarters make a whole!

question.text: A cake is cut into 4 equal pieces. What fraction is one piece?
question.explanation: One quarter (¼)! When something is divided into 4 equal parts, each part is a quarter.

1.title: Classifying Animals by Features
1.tip: Scientists group animals by their body features. MAMMALS have fur or hair and feed milk. BIRDS have feathers, beaks, and wings. REPTILES have dry scales. AMPHIBIANS have moist skin and live in water and on land. FISH have scales, fins, and gills!

question.text: Which feature do ALL birds share?
question.explanation: Feathers! Every bird — from tiny wrens to huge emus — has feathers. It is the defining feature of birds.

1.title: What Plants Need to Survive
1.tip: Plants are living things that need WATER (absorbed by roots), SUNLIGHT (caught by leaves to make food), AIR (carbon dioxide enters through tiny leaf holes), and SOIL (provides nutrients and anchor). Remove any one and the plant suffers!

question.text: A plant kept in a dark cupboard with water will…
options: Not grow well — it needs sunlight
question.explanation: Not grow well! Plants need sunlight to make food through photosynthesis. Without light, they become weak and pale.

1.title: Living Things and Their Habitats
1.tip: A HABITAT is the natural environment where a plant or animal lives. It provides everything the organism needs: food, water, shelter, and space. A pond is a habitat for frogs. A eucalyptus forest is a habitat for koalas. If the habitat is damaged, the animals suffer!

question.text: What does a habitat provide for living things?
question.explanation: A habitat provides food, water, shelter, AND space — everything a living thing needs to survive!

1.title: Special Features of Australia
1.tip: Australia is the driest inhabited continent on Earth! It has unique features: the Great Barrier Reef (world's largest coral reef), Uluru (a sacred rock formation), vast deserts called the Outback, and tropical rainforests in the north. No other continent has such diversity!

question.text: What is the Great Barrier Reef made of?
question.explanation: Living coral! The Great Barrier Reef is built by tiny animals called coral polyps and is so large it can be seen from space.

1.title: Mountains, Rivers, and Coasts
1.tip: Earth's surface has many landforms! MOUNTAINS are high and steep. VALLEYS are low areas between mountains. RIVERS carry fresh water from mountains to the sea. COASTS are where the land meets the ocean. Australia's coastline stretches over 25,000 km!

question.text: What is a COAST?
question.explanation: Where the land meets the ocean! Australia is famous for its beautiful coastline and beaches.

1.title: Light Sources and Shadows
1.tip: LIGHT comes from sources like the Sun, torches, and light bulbs. Light travels in straight lines! When an object blocks light, a SHADOW forms on the other side. Shadows change size: close to the light = BIG shadow. Far from the light = SMALL shadow!

question.text: Why does your shadow change size during the day?
options: Because the Sun changes position in the sky
question.explanation: The Sun moves across the sky! When the Sun is low (morning/evening), shadows are long. When high (midday), shadows are short.

1.title: What Makes Things Work
1.tip: ENERGY is needed to make things happen! Batteries store energy for torches and toys. Food gives your body energy to move and think. The Sun provides light and heat energy. Electricity powers lights, computers, and fridges. Without energy, nothing works!

question.text: Where does your body get the energy it needs to run and play?
question.explanation: From food! Your body breaks down food to release the energy your muscles and brain need.

1.title: Solids, Liquids, and Gases
1.tip: Everything around you is a SOLID, LIQUID, or GAS! Solids keep their shape (like a rock or a chair). Liquids flow and take the shape of their container (like water or juice). Gases spread out to fill all available space (like the air you breathe).

question.text: Which is a GAS?
question.explanation: The air we breathe is a gas! Gases are invisible, spread out everywhere, and don't have a fixed shape.

1.title: Heating and Cooling Materials
1.tip: Heat can CHANGE materials! Heating ice turns it into water (melting). Heating water turns it into steam (evaporating). Cooling water turns it back into ice (freezing). Heating bread makes toast — but you can't un-toast bread! Some changes are reversible, some are not.

question.text: What happens when you heat ice?
question.explanation: It melts! Heat energy makes the ice particles move faster and the solid ice becomes liquid water.

1.title: Choosing Kindness
1.tip: Every day you face choices. KIND choices help others feel good: including someone who is alone, sharing your pencils, or saying "are you okay?" UNKIND choices hurt others. Before you act, think: "How would I feel if someone did this to me?" This is called the GOLDEN RULE.

question.text: You notice a classmate sitting alone at lunch looking sad. What is the kindest choice?
options: Invite them to sit with you
question.explanation: Invite them to join you! Small acts of kindness can make someone's whole day better.

1.title: Families and Celebrations
1.tip: Families come in all shapes and sizes! Some have two parents, some have one. Some include grandparents, aunties, and uncles. Every family has its own traditions and ways of celebrating. Respecting that all families are different is important.

question.text: Why is it important to respect that families are different?
options: Because everyone deserves respect for who they are
Only if the teacher says so
question.explanation: Everyone deserves respect! Families are diverse and beautiful in their own ways. Respecting differences makes our community stronger.

1.title: Aboriginal Connection to Country
1.tip: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have a deep connection to the land, called "Country." They believe the land, water, sky, animals, and people are all connected. Through Dreaming stories, they explain how the world was created and share important knowledge passed down for tens of thousands of years.

question.text: Aboriginal Dreaming stories are used to…
options: Share knowledge about the world and how it was created
question.explanation: Share knowledge about the creation of the world! Dreaming stories carry laws, knowledge, and cultural practices across generations.

1.title: What Computers Can Do
1.tip: A computer is a machine that follows INSTRUCTIONS to do amazing things: solve maths, play music, show videos, send messages, and control robots! Computers are in phones, tablets, watches, and even some fridges. They work incredibly fast — millions of calculations per second!

question.text: A computer follows _____ to do its work.
question.explanation: Instructions! Computers do exactly what they are told through programs (sets of instructions). They can't think on their own — yet!

1.title: Being Safe with Technology
1.tip: Technology is powerful but you must be SAFE! Never share your full name, address, or school with strangers online. If something online makes you uncomfortable, TELL A TRUSTED ADULT immediately. Limit screen time and balance it with outdoor play, reading, and creative activities.

question.text: If a stranger online asks where you live, you should…
options: Say no and tell a trusted adult immediately
question.explanation: Say no and tell an adult! Never share personal details with strangers online. A trusted adult will help keep you safe.

1.title: Adding Within 20
1.tip: When adding numbers within 20, "make ten" first! For 7 + 6, think: 7 needs 3 more to reach 10, then add the leftover 3 — much easier than counting on fingers. Try the same strategy with the numbers in the question.

question.text: What is 8 + 5?

1.title: Counting by 2s, 5s, and 10s
1.tip: Skip counting means jumping over numbers in equal steps. Count by 2s and each jump adds 2. Count by 10s and each jump adds 10. When you count by 5s you add 5 every step: 5, then 10, and so on. Use the "add 5 each step" rule for the question below.

question.text: When counting by 5s, what comes after 15?
question.explanation: 5, 10, 15, 20! Each jump adds 5, so 15 + 5 = 20.

1.title: Shapes in the Real World
1.tip: Three-dimensional shapes are solid objects you can hold. A SPHERE is like a ball. A CYLINDER is like a tin can — it has two circle faces. A CUBE has 6 square faces. A CONE has a circle base and a point on top. Look around — your classroom is full of 3D shapes!

question.text: What 3D shape is a tin can?
question.explanation: A cylinder! It has two flat circular faces (top and bottom) and a curved surface around the middle.

1.title: Comparing Mass with Scales
1.tip: MASS is how heavy something is. We can compare mass using a balance scale — the heavier side goes DOWN and the lighter side goes UP. If both sides are level, the objects have the SAME mass. Always predict first, then check!

question.text: On a balance scale, the heavier object makes that side go…
question.explanation: Down! Gravity pulls the heavier side downward. The lighter side rises up.

1.title: Collecting Data with Surveys
1.tip: We can find things out by asking questions and recording answers! A SURVEY asks a question and records each response. Tally marks help you count: four lines with a diagonal across make 5 (||||̸). Then you can show your data in a picture graph!

question.text: In a class survey, 12 students like dogs and 8 like cats. How many MORE students prefer dogs?
question.explanation: 4 more! 12 − 8 = 4. Comparing data helps us find the difference.

1.title: Telling Time and Australian Coins
1.tip: On a clock, when the minute hand points to 12 it is "o'clock" and when it points to 6 it is "half past." Australian coins are: 5c, 10c, 20c, 50c, $1 and $2. Interestingly, the $2 coin is SMALLER than the $1 coin!

question.text: When the minute hand points to 6, the time is…
question.explanation: Half past! The minute hand on 6 means 30 minutes past the hour.

1.title: How Animals Grow and Change
1.tip: All animals have a LIFE CYCLE — the stages they go through from birth to adult. Frogs have an amazing cycle: egg → tadpole (with a tail, lives in water) → froglet (grows legs) → adult frog (lives on land). This dramatic change is called METAMORPHOSIS!

question.text: Which animal completely changes its body form as it grows (metamorphosis)?
question.explanation: Frogs! They transform from aquatic tadpoles with tails into land-dwelling adults with legs.

1.title: Bones, Muscles, and Movement
1.tip: Your SKELETON (bones) gives your body its shape and protects your organs. Your MUSCLES are attached to bones and pull them to make you move. JOINTS are where bones meet — like your elbow and knee — allowing you to bend and twist!

question.text: What allows your arm to bend at the elbow?
question.explanation: A joint! The elbow joint is where two arm bones meet, allowing your arm to bend and straighten.

1.title: Continents and Oceans
1.tip: Earth has 7 large land masses called CONTINENTS and 5 OCEANS. We live on the Australian continent! The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean. Every continent has different climates, animals, and cultures. A GLOBE is a model of Earth that shows them all!

question.text: How many continents are on Earth?
question.explanation: 7! Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia/Oceania, Europe, North America, and South America.

1.title: Reading Simple Maps
1.tip: Maps are bird's-eye views — like looking down from above! They use symbols and a KEY (legend) that explains what each symbol means. Blue = water. Green = parks. Roads are lines. A compass rose shows North, South, East, and West directions.

question.text: On most maps, blue areas represent…
question.explanation: Water! Rivers, lakes, and oceans are shown in blue on nearly all maps.

1.title: Pushes, Pulls, and Movement
1.tip: A FORCE is a push or a pull that makes things move, stop, or change direction. Kicking a ball is a push force. Pulling a wagon uses pull force. A force can also change an object's SHAPE — like squishing playdough. Forces are everywhere!

question.text: What happens when you push a stationary ball?
question.explanation: It moves away from you! A push force causes stationary objects to start moving in the direction of the push.

1.title: How Sounds are Made
1.tip: All sounds are caused by VIBRATIONS — objects shaking back and forth very quickly! Pluck a rubber band — see it vibrate AND hear the sound! Sounds can be LOUD or SOFT (volume) and HIGH or LOW (pitch). Thick rubber bands make lower sounds than thin ones.

question.text: What causes ALL sounds?
question.explanation: Vibrations! When an object vibrates (shakes rapidly), it pushes air particles which carry the sound to your ears.

1.title: Exploring Magnets
1.tip: Magnets create an invisible force that attracts (pulls) certain metals — mainly iron and steel. Every magnet has two POLES: North and South. Opposite poles attract (stick together). Same poles repel (push apart). Magnets work even through paper and fabric!

question.text: A magnet will attract (stick to) which of these?
question.explanation: A steel paper clip! Magnets attract iron and steel. They do NOT attract wood, plastic, or rubber.

1.title: Properties of Everyday Materials
1.tip: Materials have different PROPERTIES. Some are HARD (metal, glass), some SOFT (fabric, sponge). Some are TRANSPARENT (see-through, like glass), some OPAQUE (blocks light, like wood). We choose materials based on their properties — glass for windows because it's transparent!

question.text: Why is glass used for windows?
options: It is transparent — you can see through it
question.explanation: Because glass is transparent! Light passes through it so we can see outside while staying protected from wind and rain.

1.title: Fairness and Taking Turns
1.tip: FAIRNESS means everyone gets an equal chance. When playing a game, everyone follows the same rules. When sharing snacks, everyone gets the same amount. Taking turns means being patient and letting others have their go. Fair isn't always equal — sometimes people need different things to have the same opportunity.

question.text: At lunchtime there is one swing and three friends. What is the fairest approach?
options: The biggest kid gets it all lunch
Take turns — each person gets equal time
Only use it if the teacher says so
question.explanation: Taking turns! Each friend gets equal time on the swing. Fairness means everyone gets an equal opportunity.

1.title: People and Places in Our Community
1.tip: A COMMUNITY is a group of people who live, work, and play in the same area. Communities have special places: schools, libraries, hospitals, parks, and shops. Community HELPERS include firefighters, paramedics, police, teachers, and shopkeepers. Everyone plays a role!

question.text: Which of these is a community helper who keeps us safe during fires?
question.explanation: A firefighter! They respond to emergencies, put out fires, and help rescue people. They are vital community helpers.

1.title: Long Ago and Today
1.tip: Life was very different a long time ago! There were no cars (people walked or rode horses), no electricity (they used candles), and no internet. Aboriginal Australians have lived on this land for over 65,000 years — the longest continuous civilisation on Earth!

question.text: What did people use for light before electricity was invented?
question.explanation: Candles and fire! Before electricity, people relied on fire, candles, and oil lamps for light.

1.title: How Transport Has Changed
1.tip: Transport has changed dramatically! Long ago, people walked or rode horses. Then came sailing ships, steam trains, bicycles, cars, and aeroplanes. Today we even have electric cars and rockets to space! Each invention made it faster to travel and explore the world.

question.text: Which came first in history?
question.explanation: Horses and carts! People used animal-powered transport for thousands of years before engines were invented.

1.title: What is an Algorithm?
1.tip: An ALGORITHM is a set of step-by-step instructions to complete a task — like a recipe! To brush your teeth: 1) Pick up toothbrush, 2) Apply toothpaste, 3) Brush for 2 minutes, 4) Rinse mouth. The ORDER matters! Coding is writing algorithms that computers can follow.

question.text: An algorithm is…
options: Step-by-step instructions to complete a task
question.explanation: Step-by-step instructions! Algorithms must be in the correct order for the task to work properly.

1.title: Earth, Sun, and Moon
1.tip: The EARTH spins like a top once every 24 hours — giving us DAY (facing the Sun) and NIGHT (facing away). The MOON orbits Earth about once every 28 days. The SUN is actually a star — a giant ball of hot gas that gives us light and heat. Without the Sun, Earth would be freezing and dark!

question.text: Why do we have day and night?
options: The Sun turns on and off
Earth spins — the side facing the Sun has day
The Moon blocks the Sun at night
question.explanation: Earth spins! The side facing the Sun experiences daytime, while the side facing away has nighttime.

1.title: Counting by Tens
1.tip: Counting by tens is like taking big hops along a number line. Each hop adds ten, and only the tens digit changes. Starting at 6 and hopping twice lands you on 16, then 26. Use the "add ten each hop" trick for the question below.

question.text: If you count by 10s starting at 4, which number do you say after two hops?
question.explanation: Each hop adds ten: 4 → 14 → 24.

1.title: Doubles
1.tip: A "double" is when you add a number to itself. Many everyday things come in doubles — our bodies have two eyes, two ears, two hands. Knowing doubles helps you add fast. Double of three is six. Think about doubling for the question below.

question.text: What is 6 + 6?
question.explanation: Double six is twelve.

1.title: Equal Groups
1.tip: Multiplication is a shortcut for adding the same number many times. Picture equal groups: if you have groups of dots, just count the dots. Three groups of four is the same as 4 + 4 + 4. Picture the groups for the question below.

question.text: If three baskets each hold 2 apples, how many apples altogether?
question.explanation: Three groups of two is 2 + 2 + 2, which totals six.

1.title: Counting Corners
1.tip: A corner — also called a vertex — is where two sides of a shape meet. A triangle has three corners. A hexagon has six corners, the shape bees use in their honeycombs. Count the corners for the question below.

question.text: How many corners does a square have?
question.explanation: A square has four equal sides that meet at four corners.

1.title: Balancing Mass
1.tip: Mass tells us how heavy something is. Balance scales compare two objects — the side that sinks is holding the heavier object. Heavy things are measured in kilograms, tiny things in grams. Think about which side sinks in the question below.

question.text: On balance scales, the heavier object makes that side…
question.explanation: Gravity pulls the heavier side down.

1.title: Making Halves
1.tip: When we cut a whole object into two EQUAL pieces, each piece is a HALF. A sandwich cut down the middle makes two halves. If the pieces are not the same size, they are not halves. Think carefully about "equal" for the question below.

question.text: Which of these is true about halves?
options: Both pieces are the same size
question.explanation: Halves must be equal — same size, same amount.

1.title: What Plants Need
1.tip: Plants are living things, just like animals. They grow, drink, and make their own food. To grow well, plants need sunlight on their leaves, water through their roots, and air around them. Think about plant needs for the question below.

question.text: What do plants need to grow?
question.explanation: Plants need sunlight to make food and water through their roots.

1.title: Animal Homes
1.tip: The special place where an animal lives is called its HABITAT. A fish lives in water. An ant lives in soil. A kangaroo lives on open grassland. Each animal matches its habitat because it gives it food and shelter. Match an animal to a home on the question below.

question.text: Which habitat is best for a fish?
question.explanation: Fish breathe through gills and need to live in water.

1.title: Push or Pull
1.tip: A force is a push or a pull. Opening a door is a push. A game of tug-of-war is a pull. Forces can start things moving, stop them, or change where they go. Work out if the action is a push or a pull on the question below.

question.text: Which action uses a PULL force?
question.explanation: Opening a drawer pulls it towards you.

1.title: Makers of Light
1.tip: A LIGHT SOURCE is something that makes its own light. The Sun is the biggest natural light source in the sky. A fire gives off its own light too. A mirror is NOT a light source — it can only bounce back light that was already shining on it. Spot the maker of light on the question below.

question.text: Which of these is a light source?
question.explanation: A torch has a bulb inside that makes its own light.

1.title: Keeps Its Shape
1.tip: Matter around us comes in three common states. A SOLID keeps its shape wherever you put it — a rock is a rock on a table or in your pocket. A LIQUID flows and takes the shape of its container. A GAS spreads out to fill a room.

question.text: Which of these keeps its own shape?
question.explanation: Stone is a solid — its shape stays the same wherever you put it.

1.title: Natural or Made
1.tip: Materials come from different places. NATURAL materials come straight from nature — wood from trees, cotton from plants, stone from the ground. MADE materials are put together by people — plastic and glass are examples. Pick a natural one on the question below.

question.text: Which material is natural?
question.explanation: Wool grows on sheep — it is a natural material.

1.title: Australian Symbols
1.tip: Every country has symbols that stand for it. Australia has a flag with green and gold colours, a special song, and a coat of arms showing two famous Australian animals — an emu and another large hopping animal. Spot an Australian symbol on the question below.

question.text: Which animal appears on Australia’s coat of arms alongside the emu?
question.explanation: The kangaroo and the emu appear on the coat of arms — both animals cannot walk backwards, symbolising moving forward.

1.title: The Big Oceans
1.tip: An ocean is a huge body of water that is different from what comes out of your tap. Oceans cover more of Earth than all the land put together — that is why our planet looks blue from space. Swimming in the sea feels different from a pool because of what is dissolved in the water. Think about what makes the sea taste different.

question.text: The water in the ocean is mostly…
question.explanation: Ocean water is salty — it has lots of dissolved salt.

1.title: First Peoples
1.tip: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples are the First Peoples of Australia. They have lived on this continent much longer than any other group — longer than almost any people have lived in one place anywhere on Earth. They hold deep knowledge of the land, stars, and seasons.

question.text: The First Peoples of Australia have lived on this continent for about…
question.explanation: Evidence shows continuous occupation for tens of thousands of years.

1.title: The Rolling Idea
1.tip: Long ago, people carried everything by hand — heavy loads were hard work. Then someone invented something round that rolled. That invention changed life forever — it led to carts, wagons, and eventually bicycles and cars.

question.text: Which invention made moving heavy loads much easier?
question.explanation: The wheel lets heavy things roll rather than be dragged.

1.title: Mixing Colours
1.tip: Artists mix colours to paint pictures. Red, yellow, and blue are called PRIMARY colours because every other colour is made by mixing them. Mix two primary colours together and you get a new colour. Think about mixing for the question below.

question.text: Which two primary colours mix to make orange?
question.explanation: Red warmth plus yellow brightness makes orange.

1.title: Order Matters
1.tip: A computer can only do what we tell it — step by step, in the right order. If the steps are out of order, the computer does the wrong thing. Think of making a sandwich: buttering the bread AFTER you put in the cheese makes a messy sandwich!

question.text: Why does the order of steps matter for a computer?
options: Computers do steps in the exact order given
Computers change the order on their own
question.explanation: Computers follow your instructions in order — the wrong order gives the wrong result.

1.title: Small Kindness
1.tip: Being kind means caring about how other people feel. When a classmate looks sad, a small kind thing — a smile, an invitation to play — can make a big difference. Kindness is a choice we make every day. It helps classrooms feel safe and happy.

question.text: Which action shows kindness?
options: Helping a classmate who is hurt
question.explanation: Helping a hurt classmate shows you care about their feelings.

1.title: Our Nearest Star
1.tip: The Sun gives our planet heat and light. Without it, Earth would be too cold and dark for anything to live. The Sun looks small in the sky but it is actually much bigger than Earth — millions of Earths could fit inside it. Describe the Sun for the question below.

question.text: The Sun is best described as…
question.explanation: The Sun is a star — it is just much closer to Earth than the other stars.

1.title: One More, One Less
1.tip: Adding "one more" moves you up the number line by a single step. Taking "one less" moves you back one step. Starting at 47 and going one more lands on 48. Starting at 63 and going one less lands on 62. Use that idea on the question below.

question.text: What is one more than 79?
question.explanation: Going one more moves from 79 up to 80.

1.title: Counting On
1.tip: Counting on means starting at the bigger number and counting up. For 5 + 3, put 5 in your head and count three more: "6, 7, 8". It is faster than counting from zero. Try counting on for the question below.

question.text: What is 8 + 3?
question.explanation: Start at 8 and count on three: 9, 10, 11.

1.title: Sharing Fairly
1.tip: Division can be sharing. If six biscuits are shared between three friends, each friend gets the same amount: two each. That is 6 shared into 3 equal groups. Fair sharing means every person gets the same.

question.text: If 8 stickers are shared equally between 2 friends, how many does each get?
question.explanation: Eight stickers split into two equal groups gives four each.

1.title: Round and Flat
1.tip: A 2D shape is flat — you can draw it on paper (like a circle or a square). A 3D shape is SOLID — you can hold it in your hand. A cube looks like a dice. A cone looks like an ice-cream cone. Think flat vs solid for the question below.

question.text: Which of these is a 3D shape?
question.explanation: A sphere is a 3D solid shape, like a ball.

1.title: Cups and Bottles
1.tip: Capacity is how much a container can hold. A small cup holds a little. A big bottle holds a lot. We can compare by pouring — whichever container fills up first has the smaller capacity. Think about capacity on the question below.

question.text: Which container probably has the LARGEST capacity?
question.explanation: A bathtub holds far more than any of the others.

1.title: Picture Graphs
1.tip: A picture graph uses small pictures to show numbers — one picture stands for one thing. If each apple on the graph means one apple eaten, you count the apples to see the total. Picture graphs make data easy to read at a glance.

question.text: On a picture graph where each star = 1 goal scored, four stars next to Liam’s name means Liam scored…
question.explanation: Each star is one goal, so four stars means four goals.

1.title: Senses
1.tip: Our bodies have five senses that tell us about the world: sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch. Each sense uses a special body part that collects information and sends it to the brain. Pair each sense with the right body part for the question below.

question.text: Which body part do we use to smell things?
question.explanation: The nose senses smell from tiny particles in the air.

1.title: Day and Night Animals
1.tip: Some animals are active in the day — like magpies and butterflies. Others wake up at night — like possums, microbats, and owls. Night animals have big eyes or sharp ears to find food in the dark. Think about when an animal is active on the question below.

question.text: Animals that are active at night are called…
question.explanation: Night-active (nocturnal) animals hunt and feed during night hours.

1.title: Slippery and Grippy
1.tip: Friction is a force that slows things down when two surfaces rub together. A smooth floor has low friction — things slide easily. A rough carpet has high friction — things grip and stop faster. Think about grip on the question below.

question.text: Which surface would a ball roll FURTHEST on?
question.explanation: Polished floorboards are smooth, so there is little friction to slow the ball.

1.title: Loud and Soft
1.tip: Sounds can be LOUD or SOFT. How loud a sound is depends on how strongly something vibrates. A big strong drumbeat makes the air shake hard — very loud. A pencil falling on a carpet barely shakes the air — very soft. Pick the loud event on the question below.

question.text: Which sound is usually the LOUDEST?
question.explanation: Thunder is caused by huge, powerful vibrations in the air — very loud.

1.title: Water Changes
1.tip: Water is a liquid you drink. When water gets very cold it becomes ice — a solid. When water gets very hot it turns into steam — a gas you cannot easily see. The same water can be a solid, liquid, or gas depending on temperature.

question.text: What does water become when it gets very cold?
question.explanation: Cold water freezes into ice — a solid.

1.title: Soft and Hard
1.tip: Materials have different properties. SOFT materials squash easily when you press them — they give way under your fingers. HARD materials do not squash — they feel firm and unyielding. We pick materials to suit the job: a pillow should be soft, but a hammer needs to be hard.

question.text: Which material is SOFT?
question.explanation: A sponge squashes under pressure — it is soft.

1.title: Using a Key
1.tip: A map uses small pictures (symbols) to save space, but the pictures do not explain themselves. That is why every good map has a small box called a KEY or legend. For example, a blue wavy line might mean a river, and a green patch might mean a park. Without looking at the key, you would not know what the symbols mean.

question.text: What is the purpose of a map KEY?
options: To tell you what each symbol stands for
question.explanation: A map key matches each symbol to what it represents on the map.

1.title: Rivers
1.tip: A river is a long ribbon of fresh water that flows across the land. Rivers begin high up — usually in mountains or hills — and flow downhill because of gravity. Eventually many rivers empty into the sea. Think about where rivers flow on the question below.

question.text: Rivers generally flow from…
options: The sea up to the mountains
High ground down to the sea
The sky down to the sea
question.explanation: Gravity pulls water downhill from high ground until it reaches the sea.

1.title: Captain Cook
1.tip: A famous British lieutenant sailed from Britain to the east coast of Australia long before Australia had its modern name. He led a mapping voyage along the coast aboard a tall wooden sailing ship. Aboriginal peoples had already lived on the continent for tens of thousands of years before any European ship arrived.

question.text: What was the name of the ship that carried James Cook to Australia's east coast?
question.explanation: Cook's ship was the Endeavour.

1.title: Writing Things Down
1.tip: Before writing was invented, people had to remember everything or tell stories aloud. Writing let humans save their words on clay, stone, and later paper. Once ideas could be written, knowledge spread much farther and lasted much longer.

question.text: Why was the invention of writing important?
options: It let people save and share ideas
It was only used for games
question.explanation: Writing let ideas travel across time and distance.

1.title: Warm and Cool Colours
1.tip: Colours can feel warm or cool. WARM colours — red, orange, yellow — remind us of fire and sun. COOL colours — blue, green, purple — remind us of water and ice. Artists use warm and cool colours to give paintings a feeling. Pick a warm colour on the question below.

question.text: Which of these is a WARM colour?
question.explanation: Red is a warm colour — it reminds us of fire and sun.

1.title: Picture the Steps
1.tip: A flowchart is a picture that shows the steps of a plan, with arrows between them. People use flowchart pictures to plan games, recipes, and computer programs. Each box is one step, and the arrow tells you what to do next.

question.text: What does a flowchart use to show the steps of a plan?
question.explanation: Flowcharts use boxes for each step and arrows to show which step comes next.

1.title: Families Are Different
1.tip: A family can look many different ways. Some children live with one parent, some with two. Others live with grandparents, aunts, uncles, or foster carers. Every family is unique, and no two families look exactly alike. That variety is what makes communities strong.

question.text: Which statement about families is TRUE?
options: Families come in many shapes and sizes
A family must have two parents
question.explanation: Families are diverse — each one is different, and every kind is a real family.

1.title: Our Spinning Home
1.tip: Earth spins like a top, and as it turns, different parts of it face the Sun. When our side faces the Sun, it is daytime. When our side turns away, it is night. That is why the sun seems to rise and set every day.

question.text: Why do we have day and night on Earth?
options: The Sun switches off at night
Earth spins, so our side faces the Sun then turns away
question.explanation: Earth rotates once a day — day and night are our view of the spin.

2.title: Numbers to 100
2.tip: Every number up to 100 has a place value: tens and ones. Look at 47 — the "4" means 4 tens (forty) and the "7" means 7 ones. When you add 10 to a number, only the tens digit changes: 47 + 10 = 57. Try the same trick on the question below.

question.text: What number is ten more than 25?
question.explanation: 25 + 10 = 35! When we add ten, the tens digit goes up by one.

2.title: Subtracting with Confidence
2.tip: Subtracting means finding the difference. A quick strategy is counting back. To work out 23 − 4, start at 23 and count back four steps: 22, 21, 20, 19. The last number you say is the answer! Use this counting-back method on the question below.

question.text: What is 18 − 5?

2.title: 3D Shapes
2.tip: 3D shapes are solid shapes you can hold! A cube is like a box — 6 flat faces. A sphere is like a ball — perfectly round. A cylinder is like a can — circles on top and bottom. A cone is like an ice cream cone!

question.text: What 3D shape is a ball?
question.explanation: A ball is a sphere! A sphere is perfectly round in every direction.

2.title: Measuring with Rulers
2.tip: We use rulers to measure how long things are! The small marks show centimetres (cm). Line up one end of the object with the 0 on the ruler, then read the number at the other end. That's the length!

question.text: What tool do we use to measure how long something is?
question.explanation: A ruler! Rulers measure length in centimetres and millimetres.

2.title: Number Patterns
2.tip: Numbers can make patterns! Skip counting creates number patterns. Count by 5s and each term goes up by 5. Count by a bigger step, like 10s, and each term goes up by that step. Spotting the step between numbers is the key to predicting what comes next. Find the step in the question below.

question.text: What comes next: 10, 20, 30, …?
question.explanation: 40! We're counting by 10s: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50…

2.title: Quarters
2.tip: When we cut something into 4 equal pieces, each piece is called a QUARTER (¼). A pizza cut into 4 slices gives you 4 quarters. Two quarters make a half (½). Four quarters make a whole!

question.text: If you cut a pizza into 4 equal pieces, each piece is…
question.explanation: Each piece is a quarter! A quarter means one of four equal parts (¼).

2.title: Backbones or Not?
2.tip: Animals are divided into two big groups! Vertebrates have a backbone (spine) inside — like fish, frogs, snakes, birds, and mammals. Invertebrates have NO backbone — like insects, spiders, worms, and jellyfish!

question.text: Which animal has NO backbone?
question.explanation: Jellyfish have no backbone! They are invertebrates — soft, squishy, and boneless.

2.title: Parts of a Plant
2.tip: Plants have important parts! ROOTS drink water from the soil. The STEM holds the plant up and carries water to the leaves. LEAVES catch sunlight to make food. FLOWERS make seeds for new plants!

question.text: Which part of a plant takes in water from the soil?
question.explanation: Roots! They grow underground and drink up water and nutrients from the soil.

2.title: Simple Food Chains
2.tip: A food chain shows who eats what! Grass → Rabbit → Fox. The grass is eaten by the rabbit, and the rabbit is eaten by the fox. Every food chain starts with a plant that gets energy from the Sun!

question.text: In a food chain, what does a rabbit eat?
question.explanation: Rabbits eat grass! In a food chain, rabbits are herbivores (plant eaters).

2.title: Amazing Australian Animals
2.tip: Australia has animals found nowhere else on Earth! The platypus has a duck bill and lays eggs but is a mammal. Koalas sleep 20 hours a day! Wombats do cube-shaped poo! How fascinating!

question.text: Which animal is found only in Australia?
question.explanation: The platypus is uniquely Australian! It's one of only two mammals that lay eggs.

2.title: Volcanoes
2.tip: A volcano is an opening in the Earth where hot, melted rock called lava can escape! When a volcano erupts, lava flows out, ash clouds rise into the sky, and the land changes shape. Some volcanoes are under the ocean!

question.text: What comes out of a volcano when it erupts?
question.explanation: Lava! It's super-hot melted rock from deep inside the Earth.

2.title: Shadows
2.tip: When light hits an object that it cannot pass through, a shadow appears on the other side! Shadows are dark areas. They change size depending on where the light is. On a sunny day, your shadow follows you everywhere!

question.text: What do you need to make a shadow?
question.explanation: You need light and an object! The object blocks the light, creating a dark shadow behind it.

2.title: Heat Energy
2.tip: Heat is a type of energy! It flows from hot things to cold things. The Sun heats the Earth. A heater warms a room. Ice cream melts because heat from the air flows into it. We measure heat with a thermometer!

question.text: What type of energy does the Sun give us?
question.explanation: The Sun gives us heat and light energy! That's what keeps our planet warm and bright.

2.title: Melting and Freezing
2.tip: Water can change state! When ice gets warm, it MELTS into liquid water. When water gets very cold (0°C), it FREEZES back into ice. The temperature decides whether water is solid, liquid, or gas!

question.text: What happens to ice when it gets warm?
question.explanation: Ice melts into liquid water when it warms up! This happens at 0°C.

2.title: Dissolving
2.tip: When you stir sugar into water, the sugar seems to disappear! But it hasn't gone — it has DISSOLVED. The sugar broke into tiny pieces too small to see, mixed into the water. That's why the water tastes sweet!

question.text: What happens when you stir sugar into warm water?
question.explanation: The sugar dissolves! It breaks into tiny invisible particles and mixes completely into the water.

2.title: Honesty Matters
2.tip: Telling the truth is really important, even when it's hard! If you accidentally break something, being honest about it shows courage and responsibility. People trust honest people. Lies might seem easier, but they always make things worse.

question.text: If you accidentally break a vase, what should you do?
question.explanation: Tell the truth! It takes courage, but honesty builds trust and shows responsibility.

2.title: Languages Around the World
2.tip: There are over 7,000 languages spoken around the world! In Australia, over 250 Aboriginal languages existed before European settlement. Some people speak two or more languages — that's called being bilingual or multilingual!

question.text: Is there only one language spoken in the world?
question.explanation: There are over 7,000 languages! People around the world communicate in many different ways.

2.title: First Nations Navigation
2.tip: Aboriginal Australians are incredible navigators! Long before GPS or compasses, they used the stars, the Sun, landmarks, and Songlines (ancient paths described in songs and stories) to travel across the vast Australian land.

question.text: How did Aboriginal Australians find their way across the land?
options: Knowledge of stars, land, and Songlines
question.explanation: Through deep knowledge of stars, land features, and Songlines — songs that describe paths across the land!

2.title: Parts of a Computer
2.tip: A computer has important parts! The SCREEN (monitor) shows you pictures and words. The KEYBOARD lets you type letters and numbers. The MOUSE lets you point and click. The brain of the computer is called the PROCESSOR!

question.text: What do you use to type words on a computer?
question.explanation: The keyboard! It has letter, number, and special keys for typing text into the computer.

2.title: Strong Passwords
2.tip: A password protects your accounts — like a secret key! A GOOD password mixes letters, numbers, and symbols (like Sunny#42fish). A BAD password is easy to guess (like 1234 or your name). Never share your password!

question.text: A good password should be…
options: A secret mix of letters and numbers
Written on a sticky note at school
question.explanation: A good password mixes letters, numbers, and symbols. Keep it secret and hard to guess!

2.title: Adding Two-Digit Numbers
2.tip: We can add bigger numbers by breaking them apart! To add 23 + 14: add the tens (20 + 10 = 30), then add the ones (3 + 4 = 7), and put them together: 37! This is called partitioning.

question.text: What is 15 + 12?
question.explanation: 15 + 12 = 27! Add the tens (10 + 10 = 20) and the ones (5 + 2 = 7) to get 27.

2.title: Skip Counting by 2s
2.tip: Skip counting is the foundation of multiplication! When we say 4 groups of 3, we can skip-count by 3 four times: 3, 6, 9. Count by 4s: 4, 8, 16, 24. The jump between terms is always the same size. Spot the jump size in the question below.

question.text: What comes next: 2, 4, 6, 8, …?
question.explanation: 10 comes next! We're skip counting by 2s: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12…

2.title: Mirror Images
2.tip: Symmetry is when both sides of something are the same! Draw a line down the middle of a butterfly — both wings match! This line is called a line of symmetry. Many things in nature have symmetry.

question.text: Which shape has a line of symmetry — both halves are mirror images?
question.explanation: The letter A has a vertical line of symmetry! If you fold it down the middle, both sides match.

2.title: Using Scales
2.tip: We use scales to measure how heavy things are! The unit we use is kilograms (kg) and grams (g). 1 kilogram = 1000 grams. A bag of sugar weighs about 1 kg. A slice of bread weighs about 30 g!

question.text: What unit measures how heavy something is?
question.explanation: Kilograms (kg) measure mass or weight! We use scales to weigh things.

2.title: Picture Graphs
2.tip: Picture graphs use little pictures to show information! Each picture represents one thing. If you see 4 sun pictures in the "Sunny" column, that means there were 4 sunny days! Count the pictures to read the graph.

question.text: In a picture graph, 4 sun pictures in the "Sunny" column means there were how many sunny days?
question.explanation: 4 sunny days! Each picture represents one day, so 4 pictures = 4 days.

2.title: Reading Clocks
2.tip: A clock face has two hands. The short hand shows the HOUR. The long hand shows the MINUTES. When the long hand points straight up (to the top of the clock face), it is exactly "o'clock". When it points straight down, it is "half past". Read the hands carefully for the question below.

question.text: On a clock, the short hand points to 3 and the long hand points to 12. What time is it?
question.explanation: It's 3 o'clock! The short hand on 3 tells us the hour, and the long hand on 12 means exactly o'clock.

2.title: The Butterfly Life Cycle
2.tip: A butterfly has an amazing life cycle! It starts as a tiny EGG, hatches into a CATERPILLAR that eats and grows, then becomes a CHRYSALIS (cocoon), and finally transforms into a beautiful BUTTERFLY! This change is called metamorphosis.

question.text: What does a caterpillar eventually become?
question.explanation: A caterpillar becomes a butterfly through metamorphosis! It's one of nature's most magical transformations.

2.title: Our Skeleton
2.tip: Inside your body is a skeleton made of 206 bones! Your skeleton holds you up (like a frame), protects soft organs (your skull protects your brain!), and helps you move. Without bones, you'd be like a jellyfish!

question.text: What is the hard frame inside your body called?
question.explanation: Your skeleton! It's made of 206 bones that support and protect your body.

2.title: The Seven Continents
2.tip: Earth has 7 large land masses called continents: Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, and South America. Asia is the biggest! Antarctica is the coldest! We live on the Australian continent!

question.text: How many continents are there on Earth?
question.explanation: There are 7 continents! Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, and South America.

2.title: Compass Directions
2.tip: A compass helps us find direction! The four main directions are: North (up), South (down), East (right), and West (left). Remember: Never Eat Soggy Weetbix — N, E, S, W going clockwise!

question.text: Which direction does a compass needle always point?
question.explanation: A compass needle points North! That's how explorers and travellers find their way.

2.title: Gravity
2.tip: Gravity is an invisible force that pulls everything towards the ground! When you jump, gravity pulls you back down. When you drop a ball, gravity makes it fall. Without gravity, we'd all float away into space!

question.text: Why does a ball fall when you drop it?
question.explanation: Gravity! It's the force that pulls everything towards the centre of the Earth.

2.title: Vibrations Make Sound
2.tip: All sounds are made by vibrations — things shaking back and forth very quickly! When you pluck a guitar string, it vibrates and makes a sound. When you talk, your vocal cords vibrate! No vibration = no sound.

question.text: What causes sound?
question.explanation: Vibrations! Sounds are made when objects vibrate (shake rapidly back and forth).

2.title: Magnetic Poles
2.tip: Every magnet has two ends called poles — a NORTH pole and a SOUTH pole. Opposite poles attract (pull together): North + South = stick! Same poles repel (push apart): North + North = push away!

question.text: What happens when two North poles of magnets are put together?
question.explanation: They push apart! Same poles (N+N or S+S) repel each other. Only opposite poles attract!

2.title: Natural and Made by Humans
2.tip: Some materials come from nature: wood from trees, wool from sheep, cotton from plants, stone from the ground. Other materials are made by humans: plastic, glass, nylon, and concrete. We call these manufactured!

question.text: Which material comes from nature?
question.explanation: Wood comes from trees — it's a natural material! Trees grow in forests without human help.

2.title: Including Everyone
2.tip: Everyone deserves to be included! When a new student arrives, imagine how nervous they feel. A simple "Hi, want to play with us?" can make their whole day! Including others shows kindness and makes our community stronger.

question.text: A new student joins your class. What's the kindest thing to do?
question.explanation: Invite them to play! Everyone feels better when they're welcomed and included.

2.title: Rules Keep Us Safe
2.tip: Rules exist to keep everyone safe and happy! At school, rules like "walk in the corridors" prevent accidents. At home, rules help families work together. Even games have rules — they make things fair for everyone!

question.text: Why do we have rules at school?
options: To keep everyone safe and happy
question.explanation: Rules keep us safe and happy! They help everyone know what to expect and how to treat each other.

2.title: Ancient Egypt
2.tip: Ancient Egypt was an amazing civilisation! They built enormous pyramids as tombs for their pharaohs (kings). They wrote using hieroglyphics (picture writing) and lived along the Nile River. The pyramids are over 4,500 years old!

question.text: What famous ancient structures were built in Egypt?
question.explanation: The Pyramids! They were built as tombs for Egyptian pharaohs and are one of the wonders of the ancient world.

2.title: The First Aeroplanes
2.tip: In 1903, Wilbur and Orville Wright built and flew the first successful powered aeroplane! Their first flight lasted only 12 seconds and covered 36 metres — shorter than a cricket pitch! Now planes fly around the world!

question.text: Who built and flew the first successful aeroplane?
question.explanation: The Wright Brothers — Wilbur and Orville — made history in 1903 with powered flight!

2.title: Step-by-Step Sequences
2.tip: In coding, a SEQUENCE is a set of instructions in a specific order. Like making a sandwich: 1) Get bread, 2) Add filling, 3) Close the sandwich. If you do steps out of order, things go wrong! Computers follow sequences exactly.

question.text: Before making a sandwich, what important step should you do first?
question.explanation: Wash your hands first! Hygiene is important, and in coding, getting the sequence right matters!

2.title: Our Solar System
2.tip: Our Solar System has 8 planets orbiting (going around) the Sun! Starting closest: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune. My Very Excited Mum Just Served Us Nachos — that helps you remember!

question.text: How many planets are in our Solar System?
question.explanation: 8 planets! Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.

3.title: Hundreds, Tens, and Ones
3.tip: Place value tells us how much each digit is really worth, based on its position. In the number 487, the "4" sits in the hundreds column so it is worth 400, the "8" is in the tens column so it is worth 80, and the "7" in the ones column is just worth 7. Use the column rule to work out the value asked in the question below.

question.text: What is the value of the 3 in the number 352?
question.explanation: The 3 is in the hundreds place, so it's worth 300!

3.title: Subtraction with Regrouping
3.tip: Sometimes the top digit is smaller, so we REGROUP (borrow). Example: for 53 − 29, the ones place is 3 − 9 which needs help, so swap 1 ten for 10 ones → 13 − 9 in the ones, and 4 − 2 in the tens. Use regrouping to work out the question below.

question.text: What is 42 − 17?
question.explanation: 42 − 17 = 25! We regroup: 12 − 7 = 5 in the ones, and 3 − 1 = 2 in the tens.

3.title: Right Angles
3.tip: An angle is formed where two lines meet. A RIGHT ANGLE is exactly 90° — like the corner of a book or a door frame. We mark right angles with a small square in the corner. Rectangles have 4 right angles!

question.text: How many right angles does a rectangle have?
question.explanation: A rectangle has 4 right angles — one at each corner, each exactly 90°!

3.title: Distance Around
3.tip: The PERIMETER is the total distance around the outside of a shape. To find it, add up all the side lengths! A square with 5 cm sides has a perimeter of 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 20 cm. Walk around a footy oval — that's its perimeter!

question.text: A square has sides of 5 cm. What is its perimeter?
question.explanation: 20 cm! Perimeter = 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 20 cm. Add all four sides!

3.title: Growing Number Patterns
3.tip: Some patterns grow by DOUBLING — each term is twice the one before. For example, 7, 14, 28, 56 doubles every step. Other patterns grow by ADDING a fixed amount: 9, 18, 27, 36 adds 9 each time. Identifying the rule is the key. Spot the rule in the question below.

question.text: What comes next in the pattern: 1, 2, 4, 8, …?

3.title: Comparing Fractions
3.tip: Which is bigger: ½ or ¼? Think of pizza! If you cut a pizza into 2 pieces (halves), each piece is BIG. If you cut it into 4 pieces (quarters), each piece is SMALLER. So ½ is bigger than ¼!

question.text: Which fraction is bigger: ½ or ¼?
question.explanation: ½ is bigger! Fewer pieces means each piece is larger. Half a pizza is more than a quarter.

3.title: Mammals
3.tip: Mammals are warm-blooded animals with fur or hair! They breathe air with lungs and feed milk to their babies. Dogs, cats, horses, whales, and even bats are mammals. Yes — whales and bats are mammals too!

question.text: Which is NOT a mammal?
question.explanation: Snakes are reptiles, not mammals! They have scales, are cold-blooded, and don't feed milk to babies.

3.title: Photosynthesis — Plant Food!
3.tip: Plants make their own food through PHOTOSYNTHESIS! Their leaves catch sunlight, their roots drink water, and they take in carbon dioxide from the air. They mix these together to make sugar (food) and release oxygen for us to breathe!

question.text: What do plants use to make their food?
question.explanation: Plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide from the air to make food through photosynthesis!

3.title: Animal Habitats
3.tip: A habitat is the natural home of an animal! Fish live in water (aquatic habitat), camels live in deserts (arid habitat), monkeys live in forests (forest habitat), and penguins live in icy places (polar habitat). Each animal is suited to its home!

question.text: What is a fish's natural habitat?
question.explanation: Water! Fish live in aquatic habitats — rivers, lakes, and oceans. They breathe through gills.

3.title: Australian States and Territories
3.tip: Australia has 6 states and 2 territories! The states are NSW, VIC, QLD, SA, WA, and TAS. The territories are the ACT and NT. The capital city is CANBERRA (not Sydney!) — it's in the ACT.

question.text: What is the capital city of Australia?
question.explanation: Canberra! Many people think it's Sydney, but Canberra was specially built to be the capital city.

3.title: Weathering and Erosion
3.tip: Over thousands of years, wind, water, and ice slowly break down and reshape rocks and land. This is called WEATHERING and EROSION. Rivers carve valleys, waves shape coastlines, and wind sculpts desert rocks into amazing shapes!

question.text: What causes rocks to slowly break down and change shape over time?
options: Weathering from wind, water, and ice
question.explanation: Weathering! Wind, water, and ice slowly break down rocks over thousands of years.

3.title: See-Through or Not?
3.tip: Materials behave differently with light! TRANSPARENT materials let light through (glass, clear water). TRANSLUCENT materials let some light through (frosted glass, tissue paper). OPAQUE materials block all light (wood, metal, your hand).

question.text: Which material is transparent (lets all light through)?
question.explanation: Clear glass is transparent! Light passes right through it, which is why we can see through windows.

3.title: Renewable Energy
3.tip: Renewable energy comes from sources that never run out! SOLAR power from the Sun, WIND power from air movement, and HYDROPOWER from flowing water. These are better for our planet than burning coal or oil!

question.text: Which is a renewable energy source?
question.explanation: Solar power! The Sun provides endless energy that we can capture with solar panels.

3.title: Where Did the Puddle Go?
3.tip: After rain, puddles slowly disappear! The water doesn't vanish — it EVAPORATES. Heat from the Sun turns liquid water into water vapour (an invisible gas) that rises into the air. This is part of the water cycle!

question.text: Where does a puddle go on a hot day?
question.explanation: It evaporates! The Sun's heat turns the liquid water into invisible water vapour that rises into the air.

3.title: Reversible and Irreversible Changes
3.tip: Some changes can be REVERSED (undone): melting ice can be refrozen, dissolving salt can be evaporated back. Some changes are IRREVERSIBLE: burning paper turns it to ash forever, baking a cake can't be unbaked!

question.text: Which change is irreversible (cannot be undone)?
question.explanation: Burning paper is irreversible! Once paper burns to ash, you can't turn it back into paper.

3.title: Being Responsible
3.tip: Being RESPONSIBLE means taking care of your actions and choices. It means doing your homework, looking after your things, admitting mistakes, and helping others. Responsible people are trusted and relied upon!

question.text: Being responsible means…
question.explanation: Being responsible means owning your actions — both successes and mistakes — and learning from them!

3.title: Aboriginal Art and Culture
3.tip: Aboriginal Australian culture includes stunning dot paintings, carved boomerangs, didgeridoos, and Dreaming stories that explain the creation of the land. This knowledge has been passed down for over 65,000 years!

question.text: Aboriginal Australians share their knowledge and stories through…
question.explanation: Through storytelling, art (like dot paintings), music (didgeridoo), and dance — an incredible living culture!

3.title: The Gold Rush
3.tip: In the 1850s, gold was discovered in Australia! Thousands of people from around the world rushed to Victoria and NSW to find their fortune. Towns like Ballarat and Bendigo boomed overnight. It was an exciting but tough time!

question.text: What did people rush to find in Australia in the 1850s?
question.explanation: Gold! The Australian Gold Rush of the 1850s brought thousands of people from all over the world.

3.title: Inputs and Outputs
3.tip: Computers have INPUTS (information going IN) and OUTPUTS (information coming OUT). A keyboard and mouse are inputs — you put information in. A screen and speakers are outputs — the computer sends information out to you!

question.text: Which is an INPUT device (sends info to the computer)?
question.explanation: A keyboard is an input device! When you type, you're putting information INTO the computer.

3.title: The Internet Connects Us
3.tip: The INTERNET is a giant network of computers connected all around the world! It lets us send emails, watch videos, search for information, and talk to people far away. Billions of devices are connected to it!

question.text: The internet connects…
question.explanation: Computers around the world! The internet is a massive network connecting billions of devices globally.

3.title: Adding Three-Digit Numbers
3.tip: To add big numbers like 245 + 132, line them up by place value! Add the ones: 5+2=7. Add the tens: 4+3=7. Add the hundreds: 2+1=3. Answer: 377! Always start from the ones column.

question.text: What is 123 + 456?

3.title: Times Tables
3.tip: Multiplication is a quick way to add equal groups. 5 × 2 means 5 groups of 2, or 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2. You can also think of it as 2 groups of 5. Knowing your times tables is like having a superpower! Work out the question using equal groups.

question.text: What is 4 × 3?
question.explanation: 4 × 3 = 12! That's 3 + 3 + 3 + 3, or four groups of three.

3.title: Flips, Slides, and Turns
3.tip: Shapes can move in three ways! A SLIDE (translation) moves the shape without turning it. A FLIP (reflection) creates a mirror image. A TURN (rotation) spins the shape around a point. Try it with a book!

question.text: When you flip a shape over a line to make a mirror image, it's called a…
question.explanation: A reflection (flip)! The shape looks like its mirror image on the other side of the line.

3.title: Litres and Millilitres
3.tip: We measure liquids in LITRES (L) for big amounts and MILLILITRES (mL) for small amounts. A teaspoon holds only a few mL. A bathtub holds many, many litres. Remember: a litre is made of a thousand millilitres piled together. Use that relationship to answer the question below.

question.text: How many millilitres are the same as one-quarter of a litre?
question.explanation: One litre is 1000 mL, so a quarter of a litre is 1000 ÷ 4 = 250 mL.

3.title: Reading Bar Graphs
3.tip: A bar graph uses bars of different heights to show data! The taller the bar, the bigger the number it represents. The scale on the side tells you what each height means. Bar graphs make comparing data easy!

question.text: In a bar graph, a taller bar means…
question.explanation: A taller bar means more! The height of each bar shows the size of that category's value.

3.title: Time to Five Minutes
3.tip: The minute hand moves around the clock in 5-minute jumps! When it points to 1, it's 5 minutes past. When it points to 3, it's 15 minutes past (quarter past). When it points to 6, it's 30 minutes past (half past).

question.text: On a clock, when the minute hand points to 3, how many minutes past the hour is it?
question.explanation: 15 minutes past! Each number on the clock face represents 5 minutes: 3 × 5 = 15.

3.title: Animal Adaptations
3.tip: Animals have special features that help them survive! Polar bears have thick white fur (warmth + camouflage), giraffes have long necks (reaching high leaves), and chameleons change colour (hiding from predators). These are called adaptations!

question.text: Why do polar bears have thick, white fur?
options: To stay warm and blend in with snow
question.explanation: Thick fur keeps them warm in the freezing Arctic, and the white colour helps them blend in with the snow!

3.title: The Digestive System
3.tip: When you eat food, it goes on an amazing journey! You chew it in your MOUTH, it slides down your OESOPHAGUS, gets churned in your STOMACH with acid, nutrients are absorbed in your INTESTINES, and waste exits. The whole trip takes about 24 hours!

question.text: After you swallow food, where does it go next?
question.explanation: The stomach! Food goes from your mouth, down the oesophagus, and into the stomach where acid helps break it down.

3.title: The Five Oceans
3.tip: Earth has 5 oceans! The PACIFIC (largest), ATLANTIC (second largest), INDIAN (warmest), SOUTHERN (around Antarctica), and ARCTIC (smallest, frozen). Oceans cover over 70% of Earth's surface — that's a LOT of water!

question.text: What is the largest ocean on Earth?
question.explanation: The Pacific Ocean is the largest! It covers more area than all the land on Earth combined!

3.title: Map Keys and Legends
3.tip: A map legend (or key) explains the symbols and colours used on a map. Blue usually means water, green means forests or parks, and tiny symbols represent buildings, roads, or landmarks. Always check the legend first!

question.text: On a map, what does a blue area usually represent?
question.explanation: Blue represents water! Rivers, lakes, and oceans are shown in blue on most maps.

3.title: The Force of Friction
3.tip: FRICTION is a force that slows things down when surfaces rub together! Rough surfaces (like sandpaper) have lots of friction. Smooth surfaces (like ice) have little friction. Your shoe soles use friction to grip the ground!

question.text: Why do your shoes grip the ground and not slip everywhere?
question.explanation: Friction! The rough surface of your shoes grips the ground, preventing you from sliding.

3.title: Pitch and Volume
3.tip: Sounds have PITCH (high or low) and VOLUME (loud or soft). A small, thin string vibrates fast = HIGH pitch. A big, thick string vibrates slowly = LOW pitch. Hitting a drum hard = LOUD. Tapping it gently = SOFT.

question.text: A small, thin guitar string makes what kind of sound?
question.explanation: A high-pitched sound! Thin strings vibrate faster, creating higher-pitched notes.

3.title: What Magnets Attract
3.tip: Magnets don't attract everything — only certain metals! Iron, steel, nickel, and cobalt are magnetic. Aluminium, gold, silver, and copper are NOT magnetic. That's why a fridge magnet sticks to the steel fridge but not to a wooden door!

question.text: Which metal is attracted to magnets?
question.explanation: Iron is magnetic! Magnets attract iron, steel, nickel, and cobalt — but not all metals.

3.title: Conductors and Insulators
3.tip: CONDUCTORS let heat and electricity flow through them — metals are great conductors! INSULATORS block heat and electricity — rubber, wood, and plastic are good insulators. That's why saucepan handles are often plastic!

question.text: Which material conducts (carries) electricity well?
question.explanation: Metal is an excellent conductor! That's why wires are made of metal — electricity flows through them easily.

3.title: Understanding Others' Feelings
3.tip: EMPATHY means understanding how someone else feels — putting yourself in their shoes. If your friend drops their lunch, think about how YOU would feel. Empathy helps us be kind, caring, and supportive of each other.

question.text: Empathy means…
question.explanation: Empathy is understanding how others feel! It helps us be kind and supportive friends.

3.title: Everyone Gets a Say
3.tip: In a DEMOCRACY, everyone gets to have a say! People vote to choose their leaders and make important decisions. Australia is a democracy — adults vote in elections to choose who runs the country. Every voice matters!

question.text: In a democracy, how are decisions usually made?
question.explanation: Voting! In a democracy, people vote to make decisions and choose leaders. Everyone's vote counts equally.

3.title: Ancient Greece
3.tip: Ancient Greece gave us SO much! They invented the Olympic Games (776 BC), democracy (people voting), and philosophy (deep thinking). Greek myths about Zeus, Athena, and Hercules are still told today!

question.text: Where did the Olympic Games first begin?
question.explanation: Ancient Greece! The first Olympics were held in Olympia, Greece, in 776 BC.

3.title: Great Explorers
3.tip: Throughout history, brave explorers sailed unknown oceans! They wanted to discover new lands, find trade routes, and learn about the world. These journeys were dangerous — months at sea with no GPS, just stars and compasses!

question.text: Why did early explorers sail across vast oceans?
options: To discover new lands and trade routes
question.explanation: To discover new lands and trade routes! These brave journeys shaped the world map we know today.

3.title: Loops — Repeat!
3.tip: In coding, a LOOP repeats the same instructions over and over! Instead of writing 'jump' 10 times, you can write 'repeat 10 times: jump'. Loops save time and make code shorter and smarter!

question.text: In coding, a loop does what?
question.explanation: A loop repeats instructions! It's like saying 'do this 10 times' instead of writing it out 10 times.

3.title: Why the Moon Changes Shape
3.tip: The Moon doesn't actually change shape — we just see different parts of it lit up by the Sun! As the Moon orbits Earth, sunlight hits it from different angles, giving us new moon, crescent, half, and full moon phases!

question.text: Why does the Moon appear to change shape throughout the month?
options: We see different parts lit by the Sun
question.explanation: We see different parts lit by the Sun! As the Moon orbits Earth, the sunlit part visible to us changes.

4.title: Rounding Numbers
4.tip: Rounding makes numbers simpler to work with. To round to the nearest hundred, look at the TENS digit. If it is 5 or more, round UP. If it is less than 5, round DOWN. Example: 485 → look at 8 → round UP to 500. Another: 231 → look at 3 → round DOWN to 200. Apply the same rule to the question below.

question.text: Round 367 to the nearest hundred.
question.explanation: 400! The tens digit is 6 (5 or more), so we round up from 300 to 400.

4.title: Subtracting from Hundreds
4.tip: Subtracting from numbers with zeros needs regrouping across columns. Example strategy for 402 − 156: since we cannot do 2 − 6, we rebundle one ten into ten ones, and since the tens is 0 we first rebundle a hundred into ten tens. Work column by column to tackle the question below.

question.text: What is 503 − 278?
question.explanation: 503 − 278 = 225! You need to regroup (borrow) since you can't subtract 8 from 3.

4.title: Types of Triangles
4.tip: Triangles come in different types! EQUILATERAL: all 3 sides equal. ISOSCELES: 2 sides equal. SCALENE: no sides equal. RIGHT-ANGLED: has one 90° angle. Look at the sides and angles to identify which type!

question.text: A triangle with all three sides equal is called…
question.explanation: Equilateral! 'Equi' means equal and 'lateral' means sides — all three sides are the same length.

4.title: Calculating Area
4.tip: AREA is the space inside a flat shape. For a rectangle, Area = Length × Width. For example, a rectangle 7 m long and 4 m wide has an area of 7 × 4 = 28 square metres (m²). Area is always measured in SQUARE units because it covers a 2D space. Multiply length and width on the question below.

question.text: A rectangle is 5m long and 3m wide. What is its area?
question.explanation: 15 m²! Area = Length × Width = 5 × 3 = 15 square metres.

4.title: Finding Missing Numbers
4.tip: Algebra is like being a detective. A box or letter stands for a mystery number. To find it, work backwards. For example, ☐ + 4 = 10 — what adds to 4 to make 10? The answer is 6, because 10 − 4 = 6. We can also write the mystery number as x instead of a box. Work backwards on the question below.

question.text: If ☐ + 7 = 15, what is ☐?
question.explanation: ☐ = 8! Because 8 + 7 = 15. Work backwards: 15 − 7 = 8.

4.title: Equal Fractions
4.tip: Equivalent fractions look different but represent the same amount. To make one, multiply the top AND bottom by the SAME number. For example, ⅓ becomes 2/6 when you multiply top and bottom by 2, or 3/9 when you multiply by 3 — three different-looking fractions, one identical value. Use the same doubling idea on the question below.

question.text: Which fraction is equal to ½?
question.explanation: 2/4 = ½! If you multiply both top and bottom of ½ by 2, you get 2/4. Same amount!

4.title: Classifying Living Things
4.tip: Scientists organise all living things into groups based on shared features. The main groups (kingdoms) include Animals, Plants, Fungi, and Bacteria. Within animals, we sort further by features: fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, mammals!

question.text: Scientists group living things by their…
question.explanation: Shared features! Scientists classify organisms based on common characteristics like body structure.

4.title: How Flowers Make Seeds
4.tip: POLLINATION is how flowers make seeds! Bees visit flowers to collect nectar. As they buzz from flower to flower, pollen sticks to their fuzzy bodies and transfers between flowers. This starts the seed-making process. Bees are heroes!

question.text: What important role do bees play for flowers?
question.explanation: Bees pollinate flowers! They transfer pollen between flowers, helping plants make seeds and fruits.

4.title: Producers and Consumers
4.tip: In an ecosystem, PRODUCERS (green plants) make their own food from sunlight. CONSUMERS eat other things: herbivores eat plants, carnivores eat animals, and omnivores eat both! DECOMPOSERS (like fungi) break down dead things.

question.text: A producer in a food web is…
question.explanation: Green plants are producers! They produce (make) their own food through photosynthesis using sunlight.

4.title: Famous Australian Places
4.tip: Australia has incredible landmarks! ULURU (Ayers Rock) is a sacred red rock in the outback. The GREAT BARRIER REEF is the world's largest coral reef system. The SYDNEY OPERA HOUSE is an iconic building. Each tells a story!

question.text: The Great Barrier Reef is made of…
question.explanation: Coral! The Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest coral reef system, visible from space!

4.title: The Water Cycle
4.tip: Water moves in a never-ending cycle! The Sun heats water → it EVAPORATES into the sky → forms clouds (CONDENSATION) → falls as rain or snow (PRECIPITATION) → flows into rivers and oceans → and starts again! It's been doing this for billions of years!

question.text: The water cycle is best described as…
options: Water endlessly cycling between earth and sky
question.explanation: Water continuously cycles! Evaporation → Condensation → Precipitation → Collection → repeat forever!

4.title: How Mirrors Work
4.tip: REFLECTION is when light bounces off a surface! Mirrors have a very smooth, shiny surface that reflects light perfectly, creating a clear image. Rough surfaces scatter light in all directions, so no clear reflection!

question.text: A mirror works because of…
question.explanation: Reflection! Light bounces off the mirror's smooth surface, creating the image you see.

4.title: Energy Changes Form
4.tip: Energy can change from one form to another! A light bulb converts ELECTRICAL energy to LIGHT and HEAT energy. Your body converts CHEMICAL energy (food) to MOVEMENT energy. A solar panel converts LIGHT to ELECTRICAL energy!

question.text: A light bulb converts electrical energy into…
question.explanation: Light and heat energy! That's why light bulbs glow (light) and feel warm (heat).

4.title: Tiny Building Blocks
4.tip: Everything in the universe is made of incredibly tiny particles called ATOMS! Atoms are so small that millions could fit on the full stop at the end of this sentence. Different types of atoms make different materials!

question.text: The tiny building blocks that make up all matter are called…
question.explanation: Atoms! They're the smallest units of matter. Everything around you is made of atoms!

4.title: Chemical Reactions
4.tip: A CHEMICAL REACTION creates a NEW substance! When iron gets wet and contacts air, it RUSTS — the shiny metal turns into reddish-brown iron oxide. This is a chemical change because rust is a completely different substance from iron!

question.text: When iron rusts, it's an example of a…
question.explanation: A chemical reaction! Rusting creates a new substance (iron oxide) that can't easily be changed back to iron.

4.title: Caring for Our Planet
4.tip: Our planet needs our help! By RECYCLING (turning old things into new), REDUCING waste, and REUSING items, we help keep Earth healthy. Every small action counts — using less plastic, saving water, planting trees!

question.text: Why should we recycle?
options: To reduce waste and protect the environment
Because we have to by law
question.explanation: To reduce waste and protect the environment! Recycling turns old materials into new products, saving resources.

4.title: People on the Move
4.tip: MIGRATION means moving from one place to another. An IMMIGRANT moves TO a new country. An EMIGRANT leaves their country. People migrate for many reasons: better jobs, safety, family, or education. Australia is built on migration!

question.text: A person who moves to a new country to live is called…
question.explanation: An immigrant! They move to a new country to start a new life there.

4.title: Australia Becomes a Nation
4.tip: Before 1901, Australia was six separate British colonies — New South Wales, Victoria, Queensland, South Australia, Western Australia, and Tasmania. On 1 January 1901 they joined together to form the COMMONWEALTH OF AUSTRALIA. This peaceful joining was decided by public votes, not war. It is called Federation.

question.text: Before Federation, Australia was made up of…
question.explanation: Six British colonies (NSW, VIC, QLD, SA, WA and Tasmania) joined to become one Commonwealth in 1901.

4.title: How Computers Store Data
4.tip: Computers store ALL information using just two numbers: 0 and 1! This is called BINARY. Each 0 or 1 is a BIT. Eight bits make a BYTE. Every photo, song, and game on your computer is stored as millions of 0s and 1s!

question.text: Computers store information using…
question.explanation: Binary — ones and zeros! Everything digital is stored as sequences of 0s and 1s.

4.title: Recognising Scam Emails
4.tip: PHISHING emails try to trick you into giving away personal information! Warning signs: unknown sender, urgent language, spelling mistakes, suspicious links. NEVER click links in suspicious emails. When in doubt, ask a trusted adult!

question.text: If you get a suspicious email asking for your password, you should…
