options: Delete it and tell an adult
question.explanation: Delete it and tell a trusted adult! Legitimate companies never ask for passwords by email.

4.title: Mental Addition Strategies
4.tip: Smart shortcuts make mental maths easier! One trick: round to a friendly number, then adjust. For 36 + 29, round 29 up to 30 (add 1), add that to 36 = 66, then take off the 1 you added → 65. Or split into tens and ones. Try a shortcut on the question below.

question.text: What is 48 + 25?

4.title: Multi-Digit Multiplication
4.tip: To multiply bigger numbers, break them apart (distributive property)! For 14 × 5, split 14 into 10 + 4, then 10 × 5 plus 4 × 5, then add the two partial products. Use the same split-and-add trick on the question below.

question.text: What is 12 × 6?

4.title: Grid Coordinates
4.tip: We use grid coordinates to find exact positions! The first number tells you how far ACROSS (horizontal), the second tells you how far UP (vertical). We always go across first, then up — like walking along a hallway then climbing stairs!

question.text: When reading coordinates, do we go across or up first?
question.explanation: Across first, then up! Remember: 'Along the corridor, then up the stairs.'

4.title: Converting Units
4.tip: We can convert between different units! 1 km = 1000 m. 1 kg = 1000 g. 1 L = 1000 mL. To convert bigger to smaller, MULTIPLY by 1000. To convert smaller to bigger, DIVIDE by 1000.

question.text: How many grams are in 2 kilograms?

4.title: Interpreting Line Graphs
4.tip: A LINE GRAPH uses a line to show how something changes over time. If the line goes UP, the value is increasing. If it goes DOWN, the value is decreasing. If it's FLAT, nothing is changing. Line graphs tell a story!

question.text: On a line graph, a line going upward means the value is…
question.explanation: Increasing! An upward line means the values are getting bigger over time.

4.title: 24-Hour Clock
4.tip: The 24-hour clock doesn't use AM or PM! After 12:59 comes 13:00 (1 PM). 15:00 = 3 PM. 18:00 = 6 PM. 00:00 = midnight. To convert PM to 24-hour, add 12. For AM, it stays the same! This avoids confusion.

question.text: What is 3:00 PM in 24-hour time?

4.title: Animals at Risk
4.tip: Some animals are ENDANGERED — meaning very few are left! Koalas, bilbies, and southern cassowaries in Australia are at risk. Habitat loss, pollution, and climate change threaten them. We must protect them before it's too late!

question.text: An endangered animal is one that…
question.explanation: It might become extinct! Endangered means the species' population is dangerously low.

4.title: Breathing and Lungs
4.tip: Your RESPIRATORY SYSTEM helps you breathe! When you breathe in, air enters your nose or mouth, goes down your windpipe (trachea), and fills your LUNGS. Lungs take oxygen from the air and put it into your blood. You breathe out carbon dioxide!

question.text: Which organ helps you breathe?
question.explanation: Your lungs! They take in oxygen and release carbon dioxide every time you breathe.

4.title: Climate Zones
4.tip: Earth has different CLIMATE ZONES! TROPICAL zones near the equator are hot and wet. TEMPERATE zones (like most of Australia) have warm summers and cool winters. POLAR zones near the poles are freezing cold all year!

question.text: The area near the equator is usually…
question.explanation: Very hot and wet! Tropical zones near the equator get lots of direct sunlight, making them warm.

4.title: Understanding Map Scale
4.tip: A map SCALE tells you the relationship between distances on the map and real distances. If the scale says 1 cm = 1 km, then 5 cm on the map = 5 km in real life! Scale helps you figure out how far apart places really are.

question.text: A map scale helps you work out…
question.explanation: Real distances! Map scale converts map measurements to actual distances on the ground.

4.title: Levers, Pulleys, and Ramps
4.tip: SIMPLE MACHINES make work easier! A LEVER (like a seesaw) helps lift heavy things. A PULLEY uses rope and wheels to lift. An INCLINED PLANE (ramp) lets you push things up instead of lifting. They multiply your force!

question.text: A ramp is an example of which simple machine?
question.explanation: An inclined plane! Ramps make it easier to move things up by spreading the effort over a longer distance.

4.title: How Sound Travels
4.tip: Sound travels through materials as vibrations! It travels through solids FASTEST, liquids MEDIUM, and air SLOWEST. That's why you can hear sounds through walls! Sound CANNOT travel through a vacuum (empty space) — space is silent!

question.text: Sound travels fastest through…
question.explanation: Solids! Sound vibrations pass most quickly through tightly packed particles in solid materials.

4.title: Magnets from Electricity
4.tip: An ELECTROMAGNET is a magnet made by electricity! Wrap wire around an iron nail and connect it to a battery — it becomes magnetic! Turn off the electricity and the magnetism stops. This is how cranes pick up metal in junkyards!

question.text: An electromagnet works when…
options: Electric current flows through the wire
question.explanation: Electric current creates the magnetic field! No current = no magnetism. That's what makes electromagnets special.

4.title: Material Properties
4.tip: Materials have PROPERTIES that describe them: hardness (how tough), flexibility (how bendy), transparency (see-through or not), conductivity (carries heat/electricity), and absorbency (soaks up water). We choose materials based on their properties!

question.text: Which property describes how bendy a material is?
question.explanation: Flexibility! A flexible material can bend without breaking — like rubber or fabric.

4.title: Rights for Everyone
4.tip: HUMAN RIGHTS are basic rights that every person deserves, no matter who they are! The right to food, shelter, education, safety, and being treated fairly. Children have special rights too — like the right to play and to learn!

question.text: Human rights belong to…
question.explanation: Everyone equally! Every human being has the same basic rights, regardless of age, background, or country.

4.title: How Australia is Governed
4.tip: Australia is a DEMOCRACY with three levels of government: LOCAL (councils — parks, bins), STATE (state parliament — schools, hospitals), and FEDERAL (Canberra — defence, money). The Prime Minister leads the federal government!

question.text: In Australia, who leads the federal government?
question.explanation: The Prime Minister! They lead the federal government from Canberra.

4.title: The Roman Empire
4.tip: Ancient Rome was one of the mightiest empires ever! Romans built incredible straight roads (some still exist!), aqueducts (water bridges), and the famous Colosseum arena. At its peak, the Roman Empire stretched across Europe, Africa, and Asia!

question.text: What were Roman roads famous for?
question.explanation: Being incredibly straight and well-built! Roman engineering was so good that some roads are still used today, 2000 years later!

4.title: Machines Change the World
4.tip: The INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION (1760s onwards) transformed the world! People went from making things by hand to using machines in factories. Steam engines powered trains and factories. Cities grew rapidly as people moved for factory jobs.

question.text: The Industrial Revolution brought…
question.explanation: Machines and factories! The Industrial Revolution changed manufacturing, transport, and how people lived and worked.

4.title: If-Then Decisions
4.tip: In coding, CONDITIONALS let the computer make decisions! 'IF it's raining THEN take an umbrella ELSE wear sunglasses.' The computer checks a condition and chooses what to do based on whether it's true or false!

question.text: An 'if' statement in coding…
options: Makes a decision based on a condition
question.explanation: It makes a decision! IF a condition is true, do one thing; ELSE do something different.

4.title: Inner and Outer Planets
4.tip: The 4 INNER planets (Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars) are small and rocky. The 4 OUTER planets (Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune) are giant gas balls! Mercury is closest to the Sun and the smallest. Jupiter is the biggest — 1,300 Earths could fit inside!

question.text: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
question.explanation: Mercury! It's the smallest planet and the closest to the Sun, with temperatures up to 430°C!

5.title: Factors and Multiples
5.tip: A FACTOR of a number is a whole number that divides into it exactly, with no remainder. For example, the factors of 18 are 1, 2, 3, 6, 9 and 18 — each one divides 18 cleanly. A MULTIPLE is what you get when you multiply: multiples of 5 are 5, 10, 15, 20… List the factors for the question below.

question.text: Which is a factor of 12?
question.explanation: 4 is a factor of 12 because 12 ÷ 4 = 3 with no remainder. It divides evenly!

5.title: Subtracting Decimals
5.tip: To subtract decimals, line up the decimal points so tenths sit under tenths. When the top digit is smaller (like the tenths in 6.3 − 2.5), borrow 1 from the ones column — that becomes 10 tenths extra. Carry that technique into the question below.

question.text: What is 8.5 − 3.7?
question.explanation: 8.5 − 3.7 = 4.8! Remember to regroup when the top digit is smaller.

5.title: Properties of Quadrilaterals
5.tip: Quadrilaterals are shapes with 4 sides! A RECTANGLE has 4 right angles. A RHOMBUS has 4 equal sides. A PARALLELOGRAM has 2 pairs of parallel sides. A TRAPEZIUM has exactly 1 pair of parallel sides. Each is unique!

question.text: Which quadrilateral has exactly one pair of parallel sides?
question.explanation: A trapezium! It has exactly one pair of parallel sides. The other sides are not parallel.

5.title: Calculating Volume
5.tip: VOLUME is the space inside a 3D shape! For a rectangular prism (box): Volume = Length × Width × Height. A box that is 4cm × 3cm × 2cm has volume = 4 × 3 × 2 = 24 cm³. We use cubic units (cm³ or m³)!

question.text: A box is 4cm long, 3cm wide, and 2cm tall. What is its volume?
question.explanation: 24 cm³! Volume = 4 × 3 × 2 = 24 cubic centimetres.

5.title: BODMAS Rules!
5.tip: BODMAS tells us the ORDER to do operations: Brackets first, then Orders (powers), then Division and Multiplication (left to right), then Addition and Subtraction (left to right). The KEY point: multiplication happens BEFORE addition, even if the addition appears first in the expression. Apply this order to the question below.

question.text: Using BODMAS, what is 3 + 4 × 2?
question.explanation: 11! Multiply first (BODMAS): 4 × 2 = 8, then add: 3 + 8 = 11. NOT (3+4)×2!

5.title: Adding Fractions
5.tip: To add fractions with the SAME denominator, just add the numerators! ⅕ + ⅖ = ⅗ (1+2=3, keep the 5). For DIFFERENT denominators, you need a common denominator first. Think of it like adding same-sized pizza slices!

question.text: What is ⅕ + ⅖?
question.explanation: ⅗! Same denominator (5), so just add numerators: 1+2=3. The answer is 3/5.

5.title: The Building Blocks of Life
5.tip: All living things are made of CELLS — tiny units too small to see without a microscope! Your body has around 37 TRILLION cells! Plant cells have a cell wall and chloroplasts. Animal cells have a membrane and nucleus.

question.text: What is the basic unit of all living things?
question.explanation: The cell! All living organisms are made up of one or more cells. They're the building blocks of life.

5.title: How Seeds Travel
5.tip: Plants can't walk, but their seeds travel far! Seeds spread by WIND (dandelions fly!), WATER (coconuts float!), ANIMALS (seeds stick to fur or are eaten and pooped out!), and even EXPLOSION (some pods pop open!).

question.text: Dandelion seeds are mainly dispersed by…
question.explanation: Wind! Dandelion seeds have fluffy parachutes that catch the breeze and carry them far away.

5.title: The Variety of Life
5.tip: BIODIVERSITY means the variety of all living things in an area! A healthy forest has many species: tall trees, ferns, mushrooms, insects, birds, mammals. More biodiversity = healthier ecosystems. Losing species disrupts the balance!

question.text: Biodiversity means…
options: The variety of all living things in an area
question.explanation: The variety of ALL living things — plants, animals, fungi, and microorganisms — in a given area!

5.title: Australia's Natural Resources
5.tip: Australia is rich in natural resources! We're one of the world's biggest exporters of IRON ORE (for making steel), coal, gold, and natural gas. Agriculture (wheat, beef, wool) is also huge. These resources drive our economy!

question.text: What is one of Australia's largest mineral exports?
question.explanation: Iron ore! Australia is one of the world's largest producers and exporters of iron ore.

5.title: Earth's Moving Plates
5.tip: Earth's surface is made of giant pieces called TECTONIC PLATES that slowly move! Where plates push together, mountains form. Where they pull apart, valleys appear. When plates suddenly slip, we get EARTHQUAKES!

question.text: Earthquakes are caused by…
question.explanation: Moving tectonic plates! When these giant rock plates suddenly shift against each other, the ground shakes.

5.title: Bending Light
5.tip: REFRACTION is when light bends as it passes from one material to another! That's why a straw looks bent in a glass of water — the light changes speed and direction when it enters the water. Prisms split white light into a rainbow!

question.text: A straw looks bent in water because of…
question.explanation: Refraction! Light bends (changes direction) as it moves from air into water, making the straw look bent.

5.title: Stored and Moving Energy
5.tip: POTENTIAL ENERGY is stored energy — like a ball at the top of a hill, waiting to roll. KINETIC ENERGY is the energy of movement — the ball rolling downhill. As the ball rolls down, potential converts to kinetic!

question.text: A ball sitting still at the top of a hill has mainly…
question.explanation: Potential (stored) energy! It has energy because of its position — ready to roll down and convert to kinetic energy.

5.title: What is an Element?
5.tip: An ELEMENT is a pure substance made of only ONE type of atom. Gold is an element — all gold atoms are the same! Oxygen is an element. There are 118 known elements. You can't break an element into simpler substances by chemical means!

question.text: An element is…
options: A pure substance made of one type of atom
question.explanation: A pure substance made of one type of atom! Gold (Au), Oxygen (O), and Iron (Fe) are all elements.

5.title: Acids and Bases
5.tip: Substances can be ACIDIC, BASIC (alkaline), or NEUTRAL! We measure this on the pH scale (0-14). Acids (pH < 7) taste sour (like lemon juice). Bases (pH > 7) feel slippery (like soap). Water is neutral (pH 7).

question.text: Lemon juice is an example of…
question.explanation: An acid! Lemon juice has a low pH (about 2) and tastes sour — that's the acid!

5.title: Being Kind Online
5.tip: CYBERBULLYING is using technology to hurt, threaten, or embarrass someone. It's just as harmful as bullying in person — sometimes worse because it can happen 24/7. Be kind online: treat people the way you'd want to be treated!

question.text: Cyberbullying is…
question.explanation: Wrong and harmful! Using technology to hurt others is bullying. Always be kind and respectful online.

5.title: Being a Global Citizen
5.tip: A GLOBAL CITIZEN cares not just about their own community, but about people and the planet everywhere! Global challenges like poverty, pollution, and inequality affect everyone. We can all make a difference through awareness and action!

question.text: A global citizen…
options: Only cares about their own country
Cares about the whole world's wellbeing
question.explanation: Cares about the whole world! Global citizens think about how their actions affect people and the planet everywhere.

5.title: The Eureka Stockade
5.tip: In the mid-1800s, gold miners in Ballarat, Victoria, were angry about expensive mining licences and unfair treatment by police. They built a rough wooden fence called a stockade and fought against soldiers in a short, violent clash known as the Eureka Stockade. Although the miners lost the battle, the protest led to fairer mining laws and is remembered as a turning point for democracy in Australia.

question.text: What were the gold miners at the Eureka Stockade mainly protesting about?
options: Too much gold being taken overseas
question.explanation: Miners protested against costly mining licences and heavy-handed police enforcement.

5.title: The Brain of Your Computer
5.tip: An OPERATING SYSTEM (OS) is the main software that manages everything on a computer! It controls the screen, runs apps, manages files, and talks to hardware. Examples: Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android. Without an OS, your computer can't work!

question.text: An operating system…
options: Manages the computer's hardware and software
question.explanation: It manages everything! The OS is the essential software that controls all hardware and runs all applications.

5.title: Protecting Your Data
5.tip: DATA PRIVACY means keeping your personal information safe! Your address, phone number, passwords, and photos are all private data. Only share with trusted people and services. Check app permissions — does a torch app really need your contacts?

question.text: Which piece of information should you NOT share online with strangers?
question.explanation: Your home address! Never share personal information like your address, school name, or phone number online.

5.title: Adding Decimals
5.tip: Adding decimals is like adding whole numbers — just line up the decimal points. If the tenths total reaches 10 or more, carry 1 into the ones column (like in 3.6 + 1.7, where the tenths make 13, so you carry 1). Apply the same line-up-and-carry method to the question below.

question.text: What is 4.3 + 2.8?
question.explanation: 4.3 + 2.8 = 7.1! Line up the decimals: 3+8=11 (write 1, carry 1), 4+2+1=7.

5.title: Multiplying Bigger Numbers
5.tip: For long multiplication, break the second number into tens and ones, multiply each part, then add. For 23 × 14, do 23 × 4 and 23 × 10 separately, then add the two partial products. Use the same grid/split strategy to solve the question below.

question.text: What is 34 × 12?

5.title: Types of Angles
5.tip: Angles come in types! ACUTE: less than 90° (sharp). RIGHT: exactly 90° (like a corner). OBTUSE: between 90° and 180° (wide). REFLEX: more than 180° (really wide, past a straight line). Use a protractor to measure them!

question.text: An angle greater than 90° but less than 180° is called…
question.explanation: Obtuse! It's wider than a right angle but not yet a straight line. Think of a door opened more than halfway.

5.title: Metric Conversions
5.tip: Metric units step up in tens and thousands. 1 km = 1000 m, 1 m = 100 cm, and 1 cm = 10 mm. To go from a BIG unit to a SMALL unit, MULTIPLY. So 2.4 km becomes 2.4 × 1000 = 2400 m. To go from small to big, divide instead. Multiply or divide as needed for the question below.

question.text: 3.5 kilometres equals how many metres?
question.explanation: 3500 metres! 3.5 × 1000 = 3500. There are 1000 metres in every kilometre.

5.title: Finding the Mean
5.tip: The MEAN (average) tells you the typical value. Add all the numbers together, then divide by how many numbers there are! Mean of 4, 6, 8: add them (4+6+8=18), divide by 3 numbers: 18÷3 = 6. The mean is 6!

question.text: What is the mean (average) of 4, 6, and 8?
question.explanation: 6! Add them: 4+6+8=18. Divide by count (3): 18÷3=6.

5.title: Calculating Time Differences
5.tip: To find elapsed time, count forward! From 2:15 PM to 4:00 PM: 2:15→3:15 is 1 hour, 3:15→4:00 is 45 minutes. Total: 1 hour 45 minutes! You can also use a number line to visualise the jumps.

question.text: A movie starts at 2:15 PM and runs for 1 hour 45 minutes. When does it end?
question.explanation: 4:00 PM! 2:15 + 1 hour = 3:15, then + 45 minutes = 4:00 PM.

5.title: Animal Migration and Hibernation
5.tip: Animals have amazing survival strategies! MIGRATION is travelling long distances for food or warmth (whales, birds). HIBERNATION is sleeping through winter to save energy (bears, bats). These behaviours help animals survive tough conditions!

question.text: Bears sleeping through winter is called…
question.explanation: Hibernation! Bears slow down their body processes and sleep through the cold winter months.

5.title: The Heart and Blood
5.tip: Your HEART pumps blood around your entire body through blood vessels! Arteries carry oxygen-rich blood OUT from the heart. Veins carry used blood BACK. Your heart beats about 100,000 times every day — what a muscle!

question.text: What organ pumps blood around your whole body?
question.explanation: The heart! It's a powerful muscle that pumps blood through your arteries and veins 24/7.

5.title: Different Times Around the World
5.tip: Earth is divided into TIME ZONES! As Earth spins, different parts face the Sun at different times. When it's noon in Sydney, it's nighttime in London! Australia alone has 3 time zones (AEST, ACST, AWST).

question.text: When it's noon in Sydney, what time is it approximately in London?
question.explanation: Nighttime! London is about 10-11 hours behind Sydney, so when it's midday in Sydney, it's roughly 1-3 AM in London.

5.title: Earth's Grid System
5.tip: Earth has an imaginary grid! LATITUDE lines run east-west (like belts) and measure how far north or south of the Equator you are. LONGITUDE lines run north-south (like segments of an orange) and measure east-west from the Prime Meridian.

question.text: Lines running east-west around the Earth (like belts) are called lines of…
question.explanation: Latitude! Remember: 'Lat-itude' sounds like 'flat-itude' — the lines go flat (east-west) around Earth.

5.title: Newton's First Law
5.tip: Sir Isaac Newton's First Law: An object at REST stays at rest, and an object in MOTION stays in motion, unless a FORCE acts on it. This is called INERTIA! That's why you lurch forward when a car suddenly brakes — your body wants to keep moving!

question.text: According to Newton's First Law, an object at rest will stay at rest unless…
question.explanation: A force must act on it! Objects don't move on their own — they need a push, pull, or other force.

5.title: Sound Frequency and Pitch
5.tip: FREQUENCY is how fast something vibrates, measured in Hertz (Hz). HIGH frequency = HIGH pitch (like a whistle). LOW frequency = LOW pitch (like a bass drum). Humans can hear between about 20 Hz and 20,000 Hz!

question.text: A higher frequency produces a…
question.explanation: Higher pitch! More vibrations per second (higher frequency) = a higher-sounding note.

5.title: Electric Circuits
5.tip: For electricity to flow, it needs a complete CIRCUIT — a closed loop! A basic circuit has a POWER SOURCE (battery), WIRES (conductors), and a LOAD (like a light bulb). If the circuit is broken (open), the electricity stops flowing!

question.text: For electricity to flow, a circuit must be…
question.explanation: Complete and closed! Electricity needs an unbroken path to flow from the source, through the load, and back.

5.title: The Periodic Table
5.tip: The PERIODIC TABLE organises all 118 elements! Elements are arranged by their atomic number (number of protons). Metals are on the left, non-metals on the right. Elements in the same column have similar properties!

question.text: The periodic table organises…
question.explanation: All 118 known chemical elements! It's one of the most important tools in science.

5.title: Justice and Equality
5.tip: JUSTICE means fair treatment for all people. EQUALITY means everyone has the same rights and opportunities. EQUITY means giving people what they specifically need to be equal. A fair society needs all three!

question.text: Equality means…
options: Everyone is treated fairly with the same rights
question.explanation: Everyone is treated fairly with equal rights! No one should be discriminated against.

5.title: Understanding Media
5.tip: MEDIA LITERACY means thinking critically about information! Not everything on TV, social media, or websites is true. Check WHO wrote it, WHY they wrote it, and whether other reliable sources agree. Be a smart information consumer!

question.text: When you see information online, you should…
question.explanation: Check multiple reliable sources! Not everything online is accurate — think critically and verify.

5.title: China's Amazing Inventions
5.tip: Ancient China gave the world incredible inventions! PAPER (for writing and books), GUNPOWDER (originally for fireworks!), the COMPASS (for navigation), and PRINTING (spreading knowledge). These Four Great Inventions changed human history!

question.text: What did ancient China invent?
question.explanation: Paper and gunpowder! Along with the compass and printing, these are known as China's Four Great Inventions.

5.title: The Great War
5.tip: World War I was a devastating global conflict fought in the early 20th century. Australia and New Zealand sent troops as ANZACs, who landed at Gallipoli in Turkey in 1915. We remember their sacrifice every 25 April on ANZAC Day. Over 60,000 Australians died in the war. The landings at Gallipoli became a defining moment in Australian national identity.

question.text: Australian and New Zealand soldiers who fought in World War I were known as…
question.explanation: ANZAC stands for Australian and New Zealand Army Corps — the combined force that landed at Gallipoli.

5.title: Variables in Coding
5.tip: A VARIABLE in coding is like a labelled box that stores data! You give it a name (like 'score') and put a value inside (like 100). The value can change: score = score + 10 makes it 110. Variables let programs remember and use information!

question.text: In coding, a variable…
options: Stores a value that can change
question.explanation: A variable stores a value that can change! It's like a container with a label — you can update what's inside.

5.title: Stars and Galaxies
5.tip: Our Sun is actually a STAR — a massive ball of burning gas! There are billions of stars in our GALAXY, the Milky Way. And there are billions of galaxies in the universe! The nearest star after our Sun is Proxima Centauri, 4.2 light-years away.

question.text: Our galaxy is called…
question.explanation: The Milky Way! It's a spiral galaxy containing billions of stars, including our Sun.

6.title: The Special Primes
6.tip: A PRIME NUMBER has exactly 2 factors: 1 and itself. Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23… A COMPOSITE number has more than 2 factors. Note: 1 is neither prime nor composite! 2 is the only even prime number.

question.text: Which is a prime number?
question.explanation: 7 is prime! Its only factors are 1 and 7. The others (4=2×2, 6=2×3, 9=3×3) are composite.

6.title: Subtracting Negatives
6.tip: Subtracting a negative is like ADDING! Think of it as removing a debt — it's a good thing! 4 − (−3) = 4 + 3 = 7. Two negatives make a positive! This rule: minus a minus = plus.

question.text: What is 4 − (−3)?
question.explanation: 7! Subtracting a negative is the same as adding: 4 − (−3) = 4 + 3 = 7.

6.title: Circle Measurements
6.tip: Circles have special parts! The RADIUS is the distance from the centre to the edge. The DIAMETER goes all the way across through the centre — it's ALWAYS twice the radius! The CIRCUMFERENCE is the distance around the outside.

question.text: The diameter of a circle is always _____ the radius.
question.explanation: Twice! The diameter goes through the centre and is always 2 × the radius.

6.title: Area of Triangles
6.tip: The area of a triangle = (base × height) ÷ 2. Why divide by 2? Because a triangle is exactly HALF of a rectangle with the same base and height! A triangle with base 6cm and height 4cm: (6 × 4) ÷ 2 = 24 ÷ 2 = 12 cm².

question.text: A triangle with base 6cm and height 4cm has an area of…
question.explanation: 12 cm²! Area = (base × height) ÷ 2 = (6 × 4) ÷ 2 = 24 ÷ 2 = 12 cm².

6.title: Algebraic Expressions
6.tip: In algebra we use LETTERS to stand for unknown numbers. A letter like y is called a variable because its value can change. When a number sits next to a letter (like 3y), it means MULTIPLY. Example: if y = 3, then 3y − 2 means (3 × 3) − 2 = 9 − 2 = 7. Substitute the value into the expression on the question below.

question.text: If x = 4, what is 2x + 3?

6.title: Fractions × Fractions
6.tip: To multiply fractions, multiply the tops (numerators) and multiply the bottoms (denominators)! ½ × ⅓ = (1×1)/(2×3) = 1/6. Think of it as finding 'a fraction OF a fraction' — half of a third is a sixth!

question.text: What is ½ × ⅓?
question.explanation: ⅙! Multiply tops: 1×1=1. Multiply bottoms: 2×3=6. So ½ × ⅓ = 1/6.

6.title: The Code of Life
6.tip: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic Acid) is like an instruction manual inside every cell! It contains the genetic code that determines your eye colour, hair type, height, and more. DNA is shaped like a twisted ladder called a DOUBLE HELIX!

question.text: What does DNA stand for?
question.explanation: Deoxyribonucleic Acid! DNA contains the genetic instructions for building and running all living organisms.

6.title: Water in Plants
6.tip: TRANSPIRATION is when plants release water vapour through tiny holes (stomata) in their leaves. This creates a pull that draws more water up from the roots through the stem — like drinking through a straw! It helps cool the plant too.

question.text: Transpiration is when plants…
options: Release water vapour through their leaves
question.explanation: Release water through their leaves! This process pulls water up from roots and helps plants cool down.

6.title: Our Impact on the Environment
6.tip: Humans affect the environment in big ways! DEFORESTATION (cutting forests) destroys habitats. POLLUTION contaminates air and water. CLIMATE CHANGE alters weather patterns. But we can help through conservation, renewable energy, and sustainable living!

question.text: Deforestation means…
options: Cutting down large areas of forest
question.explanation: Cutting down forests! This destroys animal habitats and contributes to climate change.

6.title: Aboriginal Land Management
6.tip: Aboriginal Australians practised sophisticated land management for tens of thousands of years! FIRE-STICK FARMING used controlled burns to clear undergrowth, promote new plant growth, attract animals for hunting, and prevent catastrophic bushfires.

question.text: Aboriginal Australians used fire for…
question.explanation: Careful land management! Fire-stick farming was a sophisticated technique to maintain healthy ecosystems.

6.title: Our Changing Climate
6.tip: CLIMATE CHANGE is the long-term shift in Earth's temperatures and weather patterns. Burning fossil fuels releases greenhouse gases (like CO₂) that trap heat in our atmosphere. This causes rising sea levels, extreme weather, and ecosystem damage.

question.text: Climate change is mainly caused by…
options: Increased greenhouse gases from human activity
question.explanation: Human activity! Burning fossil fuels releases greenhouse gases that trap heat in our atmosphere.

6.title: Beyond Visible Light
6.tip: Visible light is just a small part of the ELECTROMAGNETIC SPECTRUM! From lowest to highest energy: radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma rays. We use them all — radio for music, X-rays for bones, microwaves for food!

question.text: Visible light is part of the…
question.explanation: The electromagnetic spectrum! Visible light is the small portion our eyes can detect.

6.title: Conservation of Energy
6.tip: The LAW OF CONSERVATION OF ENERGY states: energy cannot be created or destroyed — only TRANSFORMED from one type to another! A car converts chemical energy (fuel) to kinetic energy (movement) to heat and sound. Total energy stays the same!

question.text: The law of conservation of energy says energy…
options: Can only be transformed, not created or destroyed
question.explanation: Energy can only be transformed! It changes form but the total amount stays constant.

6.title: The Language of Chemistry
6.tip: CHEMICAL FORMULAS use symbols and numbers to describe substances! H₂O means 2 hydrogen atoms + 1 oxygen atom = water. CO₂ means 1 carbon + 2 oxygen = carbon dioxide. NaCl = sodium + chlorine = table salt! It's like a recipe for molecules.

question.text: H₂O is the chemical formula for…
question.explanation: Water! H₂O = 2 hydrogen atoms bonded to 1 oxygen atom.

6.title: The Fire Triangle
6.tip: COMBUSTION (burning) needs three things — the FIRE TRIANGLE: FUEL (something to burn), HEAT (enough temperature to ignite), and OXYGEN (from the air). Remove any one of these and the fire goes out! That's how fire extinguishers work.

question.text: Fire needs fuel, heat, and…
question.explanation: Oxygen! The fire triangle: Fuel + Heat + Oxygen = Fire. Remove any one and the fire stops.

6.title: Living Sustainably
6.tip: SUSTAINABILITY means meeting our needs today WITHOUT damaging the ability of future generations to meet theirs. It's about balance: using resources wisely, protecting ecosystems, reducing waste, and thinking long-term. Every choice matters!

question.text: Sustainability means…
options: Using all resources now before they run out
Using resources wisely so future generations can thrive too
question.explanation: Using resources wisely for the future! Sustainability balances present needs with long-term planetary health.

6.title: Push and Pull Factors
6.tip: People migrate due to PUSH factors (reasons to leave: war, poverty, natural disasters) and PULL factors (reasons to come: jobs, safety, education, family). Most migrants are motivated by a combination of both push and pull factors!

question.text: A PUSH factor for migration is…
options: Better jobs in a new country
War or conflict in your home country
question.explanation: War is a push factor — it pushes people to leave. Pull factors are things that attract people TO a new place.

6.title: The Stolen Generations
6.tip: Between approximately 1910-1970, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children were forcibly removed from their families by the government. This caused immense trauma. In 2008, Prime Minister Kevin Rudd delivered a formal national apology. 'Sorry Day' (26 May) honours this history.

question.text: 'Sorry Day' in Australia acknowledges…
question.explanation: The Stolen Generations — the forced removal of Aboriginal children from their families. It's a day of remembrance and healing.

6.title: How the Internet Works
6.tip: When you visit a website, your computer sends a REQUEST to a SERVER through the internet. Data travels in small pieces called PACKETS, following PROTOCOLS (rules). The packets arrive and are reassembled into the web page you see!

question.text: Data on the internet is sent in small pieces called…
question.explanation: Packets! Data is broken into small packets, sent across the network, and reassembled at the destination.

6.title: Artificial Intelligence
6.tip: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE (AI) is when computers are designed to learn and make decisions like humans! AI can recognise faces, translate languages, drive cars, and even create art. AI learns by analysing huge amounts of data to find patterns.

question.text: AI systems learn by…
options: Analysing large amounts of data to find patterns
question.explanation: Analysing data! AI uses machine learning to find patterns in large datasets and improve over time.

6.title: Positive and Negative Numbers
6.tip: INTEGERS include positive numbers, negative numbers, and zero! On a number line, negatives are to the LEFT of zero. Adding a positive goes RIGHT, adding a negative goes LEFT. −3 + 5: start at −3, move 5 right = +2!

question.text: What is −3 + 5?
question.explanation: 2! Start at −3 on the number line, move 5 to the right: −3, −2, −1, 0, 1, 2.

6.title: Powers and Exponents
6.tip: An EXPONENT (or power) tells you how many times to multiply a number by itself! 3² means 3 × 3 = 9. 2³ means 2 × 2 × 2 = 8. The small raised number is the exponent. Any number to the power of 0 equals 1!

question.text: What is 3² (three squared)?
question.explanation: 9! 3² = 3 × 3 = 9. The exponent 2 means multiply 3 by itself twice.

6.title: 3D Coordinate Systems
6.tip: In 2D space, we use x and y axes. In 3D space, we add a third axis: z! This lets us describe positions in three dimensions — left/right (x), forward/back (y), and up/down (z). Video games use 3D coordinates constantly!

question.text: How many axes are needed to describe a point in 3D space?
question.explanation: 3 axes! The x-axis, y-axis, and z-axis work together to pinpoint any location in 3D space.

6.title: Understanding Ratios
6.tip: A RATIO compares two quantities! If a recipe needs 2 cups flour and 1 cup sugar, the ratio of flour to sugar is 2:1 (read as 'two to one'). You can scale ratios: 2:1 is the same as 4:2 or 6:3. Ratios show relationships!

question.text: A recipe uses 2 cups flour and 1 cup sugar. The flour-to-sugar ratio is…
question.explanation: 2:1! For every 1 cup of sugar, you need 2 cups of flour.

6.title: Chance and Probability
6.tip: PROBABILITY measures how likely something is to happen! It ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). A fair coin has a ½ (50%) chance of heads. A dice has a ⅙ chance of rolling a 6. More outcomes = lower probability for each!

question.text: A fair coin has what probability of landing on heads?
question.explanation: 1 in 2 (or 50%)! A coin has two equally likely outcomes: heads or tails.

6.title: Speed, Distance, Time
6.tip: Speed = Distance ÷ Time! If a car travels 100 km in 2 hours: Speed = 100 ÷ 2 = 50 km/h. You can rearrange: Distance = Speed × Time, and Time = Distance ÷ Speed. Remember the triangle: D on top, S and T on the bottom!

question.text: A car travels 100 km in 2 hours. What is its average speed?
question.explanation: 50 km/h! Speed = Distance ÷ Time = 100 ÷ 2 = 50 km/h.

6.title: How Species Change
6.tip: EVOLUTION is how species change over millions of years! Animals with helpful traits survive and reproduce more (NATURAL SELECTION). Over many generations, these traits become common. That's how whales evolved from land animals and birds from dinosaurs!

question.text: Evolution happens over…
question.explanation: Millions of years! Evolution is a very slow process where species gradually change through natural selection.

6.title: Your Body's Control Centre
6.tip: The NERVOUS SYSTEM is your body's communication network! The BRAIN is the control centre, connected to the SPINAL CORD, which branches into NERVES throughout your body. Signals travel at up to 120 m/s — faster than a car!

question.text: Which organ is the control centre of the nervous system?
question.explanation: The brain! It processes information, controls movement, regulates body functions, and enables thinking.

6.title: The Growth of Cities
6.tip: URBANISATION is the growth of cities as people move from rural (country) to urban (city) areas for jobs, education, and services. Today, over 55% of the world's people live in cities! Managing this growth is a major global challenge.

question.text: More than half the world's population now lives in…
question.explanation: Cities! Over 55% of people worldwide now live in urban areas, and this number is growing.

6.title: Digital Mapping Technology
6.tip: GIS (Geographic Information System) is a powerful digital mapping technology! It layers different data (roads, population, terrain) onto maps for analysis. Scientists use GIS to track bushfires, plan cities, study climate change, and much more!

question.text: What does GIS stand for?
question.explanation: Geographic Information System! It's a digital tool for creating, managing, and analysing geographic data.

6.title: Gravity and Weight
6.tip: WEIGHT is the force of gravity pulling on your mass! On Earth, gravity is strong (9.8 m/s²). On the Moon, gravity is only ⅙ of Earth's — so you'd weigh much less! Your MASS stays the same everywhere, but your WEIGHT changes with gravity.

question.text: Gravity on the Moon compared to Earth is…
question.explanation: About ⅙! If you weigh 60 kg on Earth, you'd weigh only about 10 kg on the Moon!

6.title: Racing: Light vs Sound
6.tip: Light travels at 300,000 km/s — the fastest speed in the universe! Sound only travels at about 343 m/s in air. That's why you see lightning BEFORE you hear thunder. The gap between them tells you how far away the storm is!

question.text: Why do you see lightning before hearing thunder?
options: Light travels much faster than sound
question.explanation: Light is MUCH faster than sound! Light arrives almost instantly while sound takes seconds to reach you.

6.title: Making Electricity
6.tip: A GENERATOR converts motion (kinetic energy) into electricity! Spinning a magnet inside coils of wire creates an electric current. Wind turbines, hydroelectric dams, and coal plants all use generators — they just spin them differently!

question.text: A generator converts…
question.explanation: Motion into electricity! Spinning magnets in wire coils generates electric current.

6.title: Separating Mixtures
6.tip: Different mixtures need different separation techniques! FILTERING separates solids from liquids (like sand from water). EVAPORATION separates dissolved solids from liquids (boil water to get salt). MAGNETISM separates magnetic metals from other materials!

question.text: Which method separates dissolved salt from water?
question.explanation: Evaporation! Heat the salty water until the water evaporates, leaving the salt behind.

6.title: Navigating Ethical Dilemmas
6.tip: An ETHICAL DILEMMA is when you must choose between options where none seems perfectly right. Should you tell on a friend who cheated? Helping them is kind, but cheating is unfair to others. Thinking through consequences and values helps guide these tough choices.

question.text: An ethical dilemma is when…
options: The answer is obvious and easy
You must choose between difficult options
There is no problem at all
question.explanation: You face difficult choices! Ethical dilemmas require careful thinking about values and consequences.

6.title: Supply and Demand
6.tip: In a MARKET ECONOMY, prices are determined by SUPPLY and DEMAND! If many people want something (high demand) but there's not much available (low supply), the price goes UP. If nobody wants it (low demand), the price goes DOWN.

question.text: In a market economy, prices are mainly determined by…
question.explanation: Supply and demand! When demand is high and supply is low, prices increase, and vice versa.

6.title: Comparing Great Civilisations
6.tip: Many ancient civilisations achieved amazing things! Egypt: pyramids and hieroglyphics. Greece: democracy and philosophy. Rome: engineering and law. China: paper and the Great Wall. Each civilisation contributed to the world we live in today!

question.text: Which ancient civilisation built the Colosseum?
question.explanation: Rome! The Colosseum was built in Rome around 70-80 AD and could hold up to 80,000 spectators!

6.title: The Race to the Moon
6.tip: In the 1960s, the USA and Soviet Union raced to explore space! The Soviets put the first satellite (Sputnik, 1957) and first human (Yuri Gagarin, 1961) in space. The USA won the Moon race when Neil Armstrong walked on the Moon on 20 July 1969!

question.text: Who was the first person to walk on the Moon?
question.explanation: Neil Armstrong! On 20 July 1969, he became the first human to set foot on the Moon.

6.title: Reusable Code Blocks
6.tip: A FUNCTION is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task! Instead of writing the same code many times, wrap it in a function, give it a name, and CALL it whenever needed. Functions can take INPUTS and return OUTPUTS!

question.text: A function in coding…
options: Can only ever be used once
Is a reusable block of code
question.explanation: A reusable block of code! Write it once, use it many times. Functions make code organised and efficient.

6.title: The Birth of the Universe
6.tip: Scientists believe the universe began about 13.8 BILLION years ago with the BIG BANG — an incredible expansion from an infinitely small, hot, dense point. The universe has been expanding ever since! All matter, energy, space, and time began with this event.

question.text: Scientists believe the universe began with…
question.explanation: The Big Bang! About 13.8 billion years ago, the universe began expanding from an incredibly hot, dense state.
